caption: Photomicrograph of a liver biopsy (H&E stain, high magnification) Deposits of coarse granular brown pigment are visible within the hepatocytes (examples indicated by blue circles). Further deposits also appear in portal tract macrophages (examples indicated by yellow circles). These deposits stained positive with the iron stain (prussian blue stain; not shown here), which leads to the diagnosis of hemochromatosis.

There is a solitary, well-defined, rough, flat hyperkeratotic lesion with dark spots (clotted capillaries; examples indicated by arrowheads) on the plantar aspect of the foot. Plantar warts are caused by HPV infection, particularly types 1, 2, and 4.
(A) Caudate nucleus (head) (B) Anterior limb of the internal capsule (C) Putamen (D) Claustrum (E) External capsule (F) Globus pallidus

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) - Sinus rhythm at a rate of approx. 55/min with an atrial premature beat (green overlay) after every two sinus beats (blue overlay); this is referred to as “trigeminy.” - The ectopic P waves coincide with the preceding T-wave to produce a larger, notched wave (yellow overlay; best seen in rhythm strip II) - QRS complexes are widened (duration > 120 ms) for both sinus and premature beats. There is aberrant conduction of the premature beats, with a right bundle branch block morphology: rSR′ in V1–V3 (example indicated by red overlay), with a wide, slurred S wave in the lateral leads. - There is no full compensatory pause following the premature beat (RR interval between the preceding and the following sinus QRS complexes is less than twice the normal RR interval)
A well-defined nodule with telangiectasia and central ulceration are visible on the alar sidewall.

Papulopustular lesions are distributed across the left cheek, temple, retroauricular area, and parts of the scalp and forehead. The absence of comedones differentiates this condition from one of infantile acne.

Cranial MRI, axial view (T1-weighted; with contrast) Round, sharply delimited, homogeneously enhancing, hyperintense lesion (snowball appearance) in the interhemispheric region of the frontal lobe (green overlay).
The lesion displaces brain tissue. The dura is connected to the meninges of the frontal bone (dural tail sign) by a thick, hyperintense strand. A thin rim separates the lesion from surrounding brain parenchyma (CSF cleft sign). These are characteristic radiologic features of a meningioma.

X-ray chest (PA view) of a patient with lung metastases Multiple pulmonary nodules with circumscribed margins can be seen in both lungs (examples indicated by green overlays). Differential diagnoses include malignant etiologies (metastatic disease, primary lung cancer, lymphoma) and benign etiologies (infection, embolism, autoimmune disease). An old healed fracture of the right clavicle (white arrow) is also visible.

CT head (without contrast; axial plane) of a patient with a history of chronic hypertension Hyperdense hemorrhage in the left basal ganglia is surrounded by hypodense perifocal edema (red overlay). The location is typical for hemorrhage from the lenticulostriate arteries as a result of long-standing hypertension. 1: head of caudate nucleus; 2: putamen; 3 and green overlay: internal capsule; 4: thalamus
Arrowheads: calcified pineal gland; white circles: calcified choroid plexus

Photomicrograph of a bladder tumor biopsy specimen (H&E stain) A thick papilla with a fibrovascular core (red overlay) can be seen in the center.
The urothelium (green overlay) appears hyperplastic and the normal stratification of the urothelial cells is absent.
The nuclei of the urothelial cells are large and deeply staining (nuclear atypia).
The basal cells of the urothelium are not in contact with the basement membrane (loss of polarity). These features are characteristic of papillary urothelial carcinoma.

A: premotor cortex
B: primary motor cortex
C: primary somatosensory cortex
D: Broca area
E: primary auditory cortex
F: Wernicke area
G: primary visual cortex

Photomicrograph of a colon biopsy sample (H&E stain, medium power magnification) In colorectal adenoma, the surface epithelium appears darker stained (examples indicated by blue overlay) than the normal colonic epithelium (examples indicated by hatched blue overlay) due to atypical elongated, hyperchromatic nuclei and loss of goblet cells (examples of normal goblet cells indicated by arrowheads). Villous adenoma, furthermore, show finger-like projections of the mucosa (examples indicated by yellow overlay). Colorectal adenomas are divided into low-grade or high-grade, depending on the extent of epithelial atypia.

X-ray chest (PA view) of a patient with a Stanford type A dissection Widening of the mediastinum is accompanied by a prominent aortic knob on the left side (green dashed line) and convexity in the region of the ascending aorta on the right side (red dashed line). Cardiac silhouette enlargement is also visible (black arrow line), which could indicate aortic regurgitation or pericardial effusion. Orange dashed line: proximal descending aorta
Multiple bluish-black longitudinal lesions (resembling splinters) can be seen underneath the fingernail. These lesions do not blanch on pressure.

Slit-lamp examination of the left eye There is cloudy white opacification of the entire lens (green overlay). As a result, the red reflex is absent. An additional finding is a posterior synechia (arrow), as the superior part of the pupil is not completely round.

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Irregularly irregular rhythm (examples of the irregular R-R intervals are indicated by red arrows) – Average ventricular rate ∼ 84/min (14 QRS complexes can be seen on the 10-second rhythm strip) – No discernable P waves – Narrow QRS complexes (examples indicated by green lines) – Normal cardiac axis These findings are characteristic of atrial fibrillation.

A. Transport of choline into the presynaptic neuron
B. Synthesis of acetylcholine by the choline acetyltransferase
C. Uptake of acetylcholine into a vesicle by the vesicular acetylcholine transporter
D. Exocytosis of acetylcholine-containing vesicles into the synaptic cleft
E. Binding of acetylcholine to post-synaptic acetylcholine-receptors
F. Breakdown of acetylcholine by the acetylcholinesterase and reuptake of choline and acetate into the presynaptic neuron

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Bursts of polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (VT; rate ∼ 220/min) alternating with sinus beats
– During the VT phases, the QRS complexes are wide (∼ 200 ms) and appear to be twisting around the isoelectric line, producing a spindle-shaped pattern (best seen in V1 rhythm strip; indicated by green ellipse). These findings are characteristic of torsades de pointes (TdP), a subtype of polymorphic VT. Note how the sinus rhythm converts to VT/TdP as a result of the R-on-T phenomenon (best seen in rhythm strip V6): a premature ventricular complex (PVC; red line) occurs in the downslope of the preceding T wave, initiating VT/TdP.

Photomicrograph of a vaginal smear specimen (vaginal wet mount; medium power magnification) Squamous cells with a stippled appearance and fuzzy borders can be seen (clue cells) due to bacteria adhering to the cell surface. The presence of clue cells is one of the four Amsel criteria (besides thin, white, yellow, homogeneous discharge; vaginal fluid pH of > 4.5; and a fishy odor after adding 10% potassium hydroxide solution to wet mount) for the diagnosis of bacterial vaginosis. Gardnerella vaginalis is the most common cause of this condition.

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Sinus rhythm with a heart rate of approx. 77/min (yellow overlay)
– Normal cardiac axis
– Prolonged PR interval (~220 ms)
– ST-segment depression in I, II, and V2 (example indicated by blue overlay in zoom box) to V6
– T-wave flattening in the limb leads and inversion in the precordial leads (example indicated by pink overlay)
– Prominent U waves in the precordial leads (example indicated by green overlay). The P waves in these leads are mostly buried in the U waves (example indicated by red overlay). These findings are characteristic of severe hypokalemia.
Irregular, sharply delimited, whitish (depigmented) patches can be seen on both upper eyelids and inner corners of the eye. There are no signs of inflammation.

Photomicrograph of a fungal culture (200x magnification) A cluster of yeast-like cells (arrowhead) is visible in the upper right corner of the image. Arising from this cluster are numerous pseudohyphae identifiable as long filamentous nonseptate structures (example indicated by a blue overlay). Asexual budding spores (blastospores; example indicated by red overlay) arise along the sides of the pseudohyphae. Thick-walled solitary circular chlamydospores (resting stage of Candida; example indicated by green overlay) arise from the tips of the pseudohyphae. This coexistence of different morphological types (yeast cells, pseudohyphae, budding blastospores, and resting chlamydospores) is typical of Candida albicans.

Slit-lamp photography of the left eye A fibrovascular proliferation (marked by overlay) originating from the nasal conjunctiva is seen growing onto the cornea. This is the typical appearance and localization of a pterygium.

Multiple hyperpigmented, irregularly shaped maculae are visible across this patient's forehead. Smaller spots of hyperpigmentation are visible above the right eyebrow. These findings are indicative of melasma.

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s): No discernible P waves, QRS complexes, or T waves. Absence of the normal isoelectric baseline. Chaotic irregular deflections representing disorganized electrical activity in the ventricles with a rate of ~375/min can be seen. Diagnosis: ventricular fibrillation. Note that this rhythm results in loss of cardiac output and is fatal unless resuscitation is rapidly commenced.

Photomicrograph of a peripheral blood smear Numerous crescent-shaped erythrocytes, known as “sickle cells,” can be seen (examples indicated by blue overlay; two normal erythrocytes are indicated by arrowheads for comparison). Erythrocytes with a red center and a white contouring ring, known as “target cells” (yellow overlay; cf. illustration) are visible at the bottom of the image. Sickle cells are pathognomonic of sickle-cell disease. Target cells can be found in sickle-cell disease and other hemoglobinopathies.

Chest x-ray (PA view) The lungs have a pattern of innumerable small nodular opacities. The hila are enlarged and lobulated (red overlay) and the right paratracheal stripe is widened (green overlay). Bilateral hilar and right mediastinal adenopathy is suggestive of sarcoidosis and is referred to as the “Garland triad” or “1-2-3 sign.”

X-ray chest (PA and lateral views) of a patient with pneumonia There is heterogeneous right upper lobe consolidation (green overlay) with air bronchograms (examples of bronchial walls indicated by white lines). The major (white dashed line) and minor fissures (red dashed lines) are elevated from normal position as a result of accompanying atelectasis. No hilar enlargement or pleural effusion is seen. The differential diagnosis for airspace consolidation considers the radiographic appearance, associated findings (e.g., lymphadenopathy, pleural effusion), and patient history and symptoms. Pneumonia, hemorrhage, edema, tumor, and protein can all present as consolidation on a chest radiograph. Green line: aortic arch margin; LL: lower lobe; ML: middle lobe; UL: upper lobe

Photomicrograph of a peripheral blood smear (Giemsa stain, high magnification) A typical hairy cell is visible on the right side of the image. It is significantly bigger than a normal lymphocyte and its cell membrane has hair-like projections (arrowheads). There is abundant cytoplasm and the nucleus (green overlay) is enlarged with an irregular outline. The presence of hairy cells in peripheral blood smears is suggestive of hairy cell leukemia. Bone marrow biopsy with immunohistochemistry and molecular studies confirm the diagnosis.
CT abdomen (with contrast; axial view) A large circumscribed fluid collection (green overlay) with an enhancing wall (red overlay) surrounds the pancreas (P).
There are well-demarcated, patchy areas of hair loss without scarring of the scalp.
A confluent macular, erythematous rash is visible on both upper eyelids with concurrent periorbital edema. This is a characteristic finding in dermatomyositis.

Two purpuric erythematous patches are visible. The large lesion has a necrotic center with an erythematous halo. The smaller lesion at the bottom right may represent an earlier stage. These lesions are suggestive of ecthyma gangrenosum, which is typically caused by an infection by pseudomonas aeruginosa.

Barium swallow (lateral view) The sequence of images (left: early; right: late) shows contrast (green overlay) filling a pouch arising from the posterior hypopharynx. The location of the pouch is consistent with a Zenker diverticulum, a posterior outpouching of the hypopharynx just proximal to the upper esophageal sphincter. The diverticulum occurs through a weakness in the muscular wall of the pharynx called the Killian dehiscence.

Photomicrograph of thyroid tissue (H&E stain) There is a very dense conglomeration of parafollicular cells arranged in nests and clusters (examples indicated by dashed circles). Amyloid is visible in the stroma as amorphous, pink, hyaline material (examples indicated by green overlays). These are typical histological findings in medullary thyroid carcinoma.

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Regular rhythm; heart rate approx. 180/min.
– Normal cardiac axis
– Narrow QRS complexes (< 100 ms)
– No visible P waves (these are buried in the QRS complexes)
– Minimal ST depression, most noticeably in lead I
– QRS alternans: QRS amplitude is normal throughout but oscillates (best seen in rhythm strip at bottom of image; indicated by green line) The characteristic ECG features of AVNRT are a regular, narrow-complex tachycardia without visible P-waves. QRS alternans is more characteristic of atrioventricular reciprocating tachycardia (AVRT), but may also be present in AVNRT.

Photomicrograph of a peripheral blood smear Fragmented erythrocytes, which are convex on one side (examples indicated by blue lines) and concave on the other (examples indicated by yellow lines), can be seen among the normal erythrocytes. These fragmented erythrocytes are referred to as schistocytes. Schistocytes are a diagnostic finding in patients with microangiopathic hemolytic anemias and those with mechanical damage to erythrocytes (e.g., from artificial heart valves).
The typical scarlet-colored, fine, maculopapular exanthem of scarlet fever is seen on the right arm. Flushed cheeks and perioral pallor are also evident.

Photomicrograph of a lung biopsy specimen (H&E stain; medium power magnification) An alveolus lined by a prominent hyaline membrane (green overlay) can be seen in the center of the image, containing numerous leukocytes (examples indicated by blue arrowheads) and erythrocytes (examples indicated by black arrowheads). The surrounding interstitium is edematous and interspersed with a dense inflammatory infiltrate (examples of inflammatory cells indicated by yellow arrowheads). These findings are characteristic of the exudative phase of diffuse alveolar damage (DAD), which is the histopathological appearance of the acute exudative phase of acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS).
Well-defined yellow plaques (xanthelasma) are visible on the medial aspect of the upper eyelids bilaterally. These cutaneous lipid deposits are a common finding in patients with hypercholesterolemia.
Fluorescence microscopy (immunofluorescence staining for C3; high magnification) Granular deposits of C3 are visible, resulting in a starry sky pattern.

Photomicrograph of skin tissue sample (PAS stain) Two large cells with thick walls are visible in the center of the image (green overlay). There is a single, broad-based area of contact between the two cells. These are budding Blastomyces dermatitidis yeast cells. The yeast cells are surrounded by numerous neutrophils. Thick-walled fungi with single, broad-based buds are characteristic features of the yeast form of Blastomyces dermatitidis at body temperature (37°C). At room temperature (∼23°C), Blastomyces dermatitidis exists in a mold form.

Chest x-ray (PA view) of a male quarry worker who presented with chronic dyspnea There are multiple nodular opacities present, predominantly in the upper lobes of the lungs (examples indicated by arrowheads). The nodules have conglomerated to form irregular masses (green overlay). The diaphragmatic domes are abnormally elevated indicating fibrosis (indicated by lines and arrows), which is visible as linear opacities adjacent to the shadow of the base of the heart (yellow overlay). The mediastinum is widened, indicating hilar lymphadenopathy. Diffuse upper lobe nodules, fibrosis, and hilar lymphadenopathy are characteristic of silicosis.
Frontal photo of a male patient: multiple, partially confluent, irregularly configured, sharply demarcated, hypo-/depigmented macules (white) on the trunk.

Chest X-ray (PA view) of a patient with a history of pericarditis and clinical findings of tamponade The cardiac silhouette is enlarged (globular water bottle-shape; enlargement highlighted by green-hatched overlay) due to pericardial effusion.

Photomicrograph of a peripheral blood smear (Wright-Giemsa stain; high magnification) A myeloblast, the large basophilic cell with an eccentric, slightly indented nucleus, is visible at the center of the image (marked by dashed outline). This myeloblast contains red-stained, rod-shaped granular material in its cytoplasm (Auer rod; marked by arrowhead). Auer rods are typically seen in acute myeloid leukemia.

Ultrasound left kidney (longitudinal plane) The calyces (green overlay) are dilated and have lost their normal cup-shaped appearance. Flattening of papillae and blunting of fornices can be seen. Where visible, the renal cortical parenchyma is not thinned. RP: renal parenchyma; Arrowheads: hyperechoic renal sinus

Photomicrograph of lung tissue (H&E stain; medium power magnification) There are numerous elongated, septated hyphae (examples indicated by blue overlay) of uniform diameter and with dichotomous branching at approx. 45° angles. This is the typical appearance of Aspergillus. A culture is required to determine the exact Aspergillus species.

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s)
- Sinus rhythm with a heart rate of ∼ 83/min
- Normal cardiac axis: R > S in leads I and aVF
- Normal QRS complex
- ST elevation in the inferior leads (II, III, aVF; blue overlay) with reciprocal ST depression in aVL (red overlay) and pathological Q waves in III (example indicated by green circle overlay)

Photomicrograph of peripheral blood smear Numerous healthy erythrocytes (E) and several fragmented erythrocytes (black dashed circles) with a normal convex side (colored blue) and a fragmented side that is concave or straight (colored red) can be seen. Schistocytes are a diagnostic finding in microangiopathic hemolytic anemias. These cellular fragments can also result from mechanical damage to erythrocytes (e.g., due to prosthetic heart valves).

Gross specimen of a brain (coronal section) A: thalamus
B: substantia nigra
C: caudate nucleus
D: internal capsule
E: putamen

X-ray chest (AP view) Suboptimal aeration has resulted in areas of ill-defined opacification in both lungs (example indicated by red overlay). Additionally, there is marked opacification of the retrocardiac lower left lung (green circle), with non-visualization of left lower lobe pulmonary vessels and obscuration of the margin of the left hemidiaphragm (indicated by dashed line). The overall volume of the left lung is reduced; the distance between ribs (indicated by black and white double-arrows) is smaller on the left than right, and the cardiac silhouette is positioned slightly to the left of normal (indicated by dashed and continuous lines and arrows) despite good positioning of the patient (similar distance between spinous processes and medial clavicles bilaterally; yellow overlay). The above findings indicate the presence of atelectasis, which is marked in the lower left lung. Clinical parameters would help exclude concomitant pneumonia or aspiration. Blue overlay: ECG pads

Cranial MRI angiography (maximum intensity projection; dorsal view) This image shows the circle of Willis and its branches. A: right ACA
B: left ACA
C: right MCA
D: left MCA
E: right PCA
F: left PCA
G: basilar artery No significant pathologies are visible.

Compared to healthy individuals (the “normal” loop), patients with diastolic heart failure have an increased end-diastolic pressure (EDP) and a decreased end-diastolic volume (EDV). Therefore, the pressure-volume loop is shifted to the left in these patients.

Chest x-ray, PA view, in a newborn. Mediastinal shift to the right (solid line: mediastinal border in this patient; dashed line: approximate physiologic position), bowel loops within the left thoracic cavity (green marking), and a poorly aerated lung in the left hemithorax.

Photomicrograph of an urethral smear specimen (Gram stain with fuchsin counterstain; high magnification) The smear shows an abundance of neutrophils recognizable by their typical segmented nuclei and accumulations of diplococci, in the intracellular (within neutrophils; examples indicated by white arrowheads in magnification) and extracellular space (examples indicated by black arrowheads). This microscopic finding is characteristic of acute gonorrhea caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae. Diagnosis must be confirmed by cultural isolation and identification of the pathogens.

X-ray distal forearm (PA view) A comminuted fracture (red lines) of the distal radius shows intraarticular extension and radial displacement (indicated by green lines and arrows) of the main distal fragment (F). The ulnar styloid process (USP; normal position indicated by yellow dashed outline) is also fractured and displaced medially (indicated by yellow overlay and arrow).
Left eye A small subconjunctival hemorrhage is visible adjacent to the temporal inferior limbus. This is the typical appearance of subconjunctival hemorrhage.

Photomicrograph of a lung biopsy specimen (H&E stain; high magnification) A large epithelioid granuloma containing several multinucleated giant cells (Langhans cells; black arrowhead) is visible. Dense collections of leukocytes (examples indicated by black arrows) can be seen in the surrounding area. Necrotic areas (yellow overlay) are also visible. These findings are characteristic of a caseating tuberculous granuloma.

X-ray chest (AP view) of a patient with adult respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) Confluent and multifocal airspace opacities (examples indicated by green overlay) predominate in the lower zones of the lungs. Air bronchograms (examples indicated by red lines) are visible in some locations. There are no Kerley lines or pleural effusions. The cardiac silhouette size is normal. An endotracheal tube (arrow) is present. ARDS and other causes of noncardiogenic edema can produce extensive airspace opacification, as can cardiogenic edema, diffuse pneumonia, diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, pneumonic-type adenocarcinoma, and pulmonary alveolar proteinosis. Radiography alone cannot establish a diagnosis of ARDS.

Photomicrograph of a laryngeal tumor tissue specimen (H&E stain, high magnification) Sheet-like arrangement of the tumor cells can be seen (green overlay). The increased number of lymphocytes (examples indicated by yellow overlay in zoom box) and desmoplasia in the surrounding stroma suggest inflammation.

X-ray abdomen (AP view) of an 8-year-old child A metallic foreign body is seen over the gastric bubble (green overlay) in the left upper quadrant. Its position in the bowel makes it appears oval rather than rounded on the radiograph. There is no double rim or halo, the presence of which would indicate a button battery. The appearance of the foreign body is compatible with a coin.

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Normal sinus rhythm
– Heart rate: ∼80/min
– Normal axis
– PR interval: ∼300 ms The PR interval >200 ms indicates first-degree atrioventricular block.

Sample of synovial fluid under polarizing light microscopy Calcium pyrophosphate (CPP) crystals with typical rhomboid shapes. They appear blue when their optical axis is oriented parallel to the polarizer and yellow when their axis is perpendicular to the polarizer (positive birefringence). The overall birefringence of CPP crystals is weak compared to monosodium urate crystals.
Two ulcers covered by a white membrane and surrounded by a red halo are visible on the oral mucosa. Aphthous ulcers typically begin as erythematous macules.
The sclerae show a distinct blue-grey tint.

Left outer thigh of a male patient with a erythematous, marginated lesion that has a map-like appearance and central pallor without scaling. The patient has tinea corporis caused by dermatophytes, which is usually associated with severe pruritus.

Microscopic image of a kidney biopsy (PAS stain, high magnification) A renal corpuscle surrounded by numerous tubules can be seen in the center of the image. Because the renal corpuscle is markedly enlarged, the space between the glomerular capillaries and the parietal layer of the Bowman capsule (black dashed outline) is narrowed. Within the glomerulus, there is hypercellularity due to proliferation of endothelial cells (yellow arrowheads) and mesangial cells (black arrowheads). Inflammatory infiltrate, predominantly neutrophils (blue arrowheads) and monocytes (white arrowheads), is also present. As a result, the lumina of the capillaries are not clearly identifiable. This is the histopathologic appearance of postinfectious glomerulonephritis.
Oral mucosa of an HIV-positive patient There are several distinct white plaques on the soft palate and uvula.

Photomicrograph of a biopsy specimen of a tumor located at the hip joint (H&E stain, intermediate magnification) The hyaline cartilage shows an irregularly shaped lobular structure with increased density of atypical chondrocytes with enlarged nuclei. In addition, peripheral calcification of the cartilage nodules can be seen. These findings, associated with local swelling, pain, and soft tissue infiltration are typical of a chondrosarcoma.

Photomicrograph of the gastric oxyntic mucosa (H&E stain; intermediate magnification) Oxyntic mucosa is found in the fundus and body of the stomach. The gastric pits are lined with mucus-secreting cells and are directly connected to the gastric glands, which are mainly composed of eosinophilic parietal cells (acid and intrinsic factor secretion) and basophilic chief cells (enzyme secretion).

Photomicrograph of esophageal tissue (H&E stain) Nests of neoplastic squamous cells with nuclear atypia (yellow overlay) surround prominent keratin pearls (green overlay). Components of nonkeratinizing basal-like tumor cells can be seen as well. This carcinoma is moderately differentiated and keratinizing.

Photomicrograph of a liver tissue sample (H&E stain; very high magnification) An enlarged hepatocyte (green overlay) with reduced cobweb-like cytoplasm and a prominent eosinophilic cytoplasmic inclusion (Mallory body; yellow overlay) is visible. The enlarged hepatocyte is surrounded by numerous lipid droplets (examples indicated by arrowheads). Mallory bodies are aggregates of denatured intermediate filaments and, together with intercellular lipid droplets, represent histological features of steatohepatitis.
The finger appears swollen and erythematous with blisters. This is a typical finding in herpetic whitlow, which is caused by infection with HSV-1 or HSV-2.

X-ray abdomen (AP view) of a patient with ulcerative colitis The transverse and the descending colon (green outline) are featureless, with a loss of haustral markings (cf. illustration). The transverse colon is dilated to a diameter > 6 cm and some pseudopolyps (examples indicated by red overlay) are visible. The radiographic findings are characteristic of toxic megacolon.

CT abdomen (with contrast; axial plane) Well-demarcated, nonenhancing, low attenuation areas in the splenic parenchyma extend to the periphery. The larger lesion (green overlay) has the typical wedged shape of a splenic infarction. A small enhancing vessel (arrowhead) is seen within the area of infarction. L: liver

A widespread erythematous papular rash is visible over the left inguinal region. Several individual and confluent vesicles can be seen in a T12-L1 dermatomal distribution. Additional lesions are visible beyond this distribution. This is the characteristic appearance of shingles (herpes zoster infection).

Sample of synovial fluid under polarizing light microscopy Calcium pyrophosphate (CPP) crystals with typical rhomboid shapes. They appear blue when their optical axis is oriented parallel to the polarizer and yellow when their axis is perpendicular to the polarizer (positive birefringence). The overall birefringence of CPP crystals is weak compared to monosodium urate crystals.
Right hand of an older patient Multiple scaly lesions on erythematous skin are visible. These lesions and their location (sun-exposed area) are typical of actinic keratosis.

Photomicrograph of a peripheral blood smear (Giemsa stain; high magnification) The smear shows an increase in lymphocytic cells with minimal cytoplasm and dark round nuclei. Some of those cells are fragmented (smudge cells; also referred to as “Gumprecht shadows;” red arrowheads). Smudge cells are artificially ruptured neoplastic lymphocytes and are a characteristic, but nonspecific, feature of CLL.

CT abdomen (with IV and oral contrast; axial plane) An anterior abdominal wall hernia (yellow overlay) containing fat is seen lateral to the left rectus abdominis muscle. A Spigelian hernia occurs through a defect in the linea semilunaris, a curved vertical line at the lateral margin of each rectus abdominis muscle. Spigelian hernias are usually found in the region of the arcuate line, a horizontal line that demarcates the lower extent of the posterior layer of the rectus sheath.

X-ray chest (PA view) of a patient with lung metastases Multiple pulmonary nodules with circumscribed margins can be seen in both lungs (examples indicated by green overlays). Differential diagnoses include malignant etiologies (metastatic disease, primary lung cancer, lymphoma) and benign etiologies (infection, embolism, autoimmune disease). An old healed fracture of the right clavicle (white arrow) is also visible.

X-ray pelvis (AP view) Marked narrowing and irregularity of the articular surfaces of the sacroiliac joints (red dashed lines) are accompanied by extensive subchondral sclerosis (green overlay). The findings are consistent with bilateral sacroiliitis.
Pubic lice attach their nits (louse eggs) to human pubic hair using a sticky secretion.

Photomicrograph of a lung tissue specimen (H&E stain, medium power magnification) A thick-walled spherule (yellow overlay) is visible near the center of an inflammatory granuloma (blue overlay). The spherule contains multiple endospores.
This histological appearance suggests pulmonary coccidioidomycosis. The diagnosis should be confirmed by culture and serological tests (IgM and IgG antibodies).

Well-defined yellow plaques (xanthelasma) are visible on the medial aspect of the upper and lower eyelids bilaterally. These cutaneous lipid deposits are a common finding in patients with hypercholesterolemia.

X-ray right knee (AP view) Marked lateral compartment narrowing (indicated by white lines and arrows) is accompanied by subchondral sclerosis (green overlay) and osteophyte formation (red overlay). Tibial spine osteophytes (example indicated by yellow overlay) are also visible.

The purple-bluish lesions, seen on the heel of this HIV-positive patient, are multiple Kaposi sarcoma. The lesion on the left side is elevated, while the other lesions are patches. The causative agent is HHV-8.

Photomicrograph of a vaginal smear specimen (vaginal wet mount; medium power magnification) Squamous cells with a stippled appearance and fuzzy borders can be seen (clue cells) due to bacteria adhering to the cell surface. The presence of clue cells is one of the four Amsel criteria (besides thin, white, yellow, homogeneous discharge; vaginal fluid pH of > 4.5; and a fishy odor after adding 10% potassium hydroxide solution to wet mount) for the diagnosis of bacterial vaginosis. Gardnerella vaginalis is the most common cause of this condition.
MRI head (FLAIR sequence; two axial planes) of a young child Dilated lateral ventricles are seen in the center of both images (green overlay).

CT thorax (with contrast; axial plane) A double lumen is seen within the ascending aorta. It is the result of an intimal dissection flap (yellow overlay), which divides the contrast-enhanced aorta into true (red overlay) and false (green overlay) lumens. The findings are consistent with a Stanford type A aortic dissection.

Photomicrograph of heart muscle tissue (H&E stain; 100x) Fine yellow-brown granules (arrow) are visible in the perinuclear region of a cardiomyocyte. This is the typical histological appearance of lipofuscin accumulations.

CT chest (with contrast; axial plane) The ascending aorta is dilated. A dissection flap (green overlay) involves both the ascending and descending thoracic aorta. Additional axial and sagittal images (not shown) revealed extension of the flap from the aortic root across the arch into the descending aorta, consistent with a Stanford type A dissection.
AA: ascending aorta; DA: descending aorta; SVC: superior vena cava

CT chest (axial plane; lung window) Multiple dilated airways indicating both cylindrical (examples indicated by blue overlay and arrowheads) and cystic (examples indicated by red overlay) bronchiectasis are seen in the lower lobes, middle lobe, and lingula. Several locations show examples of the signet-ring sign (illustration in the top right corner), with the diameter of the bronchus larger than that of the corresponding pulmonary artery (examples indicated by green overlay). RML: right middle lobe; L: lingula
Confluent erosions with yellow crusting are visible on the patient's chin.

Curve A shows a left shift of the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve (increased affinity of hemoglobin for oxygen).
Curve B is a normal oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve.

Photomicrograph of a brain tumor biopsy specimen (H&E stain; high magnification) Numerous ovoid structures (psammoma bodies) of different sizes can be seen, featuring a basophilic center (example indicated by yellow overlay) with surrounding, concentric basophilic to eosinophilic lamellae (example indicated by green overlay). Intervening tumor cells (meningothelial cells) are difficult to identify, which is typical for this condition. In a brain tumor, numerous psammoma bodies in absence of epithelial cells are suggestive of psammomatous meningioma (a WHO grade I tumor). If epithelial cells and gland-like structures besides with psammoma bodies, metastasis must be ruled out by further immunohistochemical tests.

Photomicrograph of a biopsy specimen (H&E stain; high magnification) Countless nests of polygonal cells (examples indicated by green overlays) with clear cytoplasm, filled with lipids and glycogen, are visible (examples indicated by yellow overlays). The tissue is richly vascularized (examples of blood vessels indicated by arrowheads). This is the typical histopathological appearance of clear cell renal cell carcinoma.
Erythematous papules and lichenified skin are visible adjacent to the popliteal fossae of this child. This appearance is typical in acute and chronic atopic dermatitis.

Giemsa-stained blood smear showing erythrocytes of a patient infected with Plasmodium vivax. In the middle, there is a red cell schizont (green overlay), which is an erythrocyte containing many merozoites (yellow overlay). Within the red cell schizont, merozoites replicate asexually every of 48–72 hours, depending on the type of Plasmodium. The increased parasite burden causes the infected erythrocytes to burst. A subsequent release of proinflammatory factors results in a fever spike. Merozoites, which have been set free from the erythrocyte, enter other erythrocytes in order to replicate again.

X-ray chest (PA view) The aortic arch (dotted line), cardiac apex (arrow), and gastric bubble (red overlay) are seen on the right side of the body. Opacification in the left upper abdominal quadrant (green overlay) is the result of a left-sided liver.

Fluoroscopy (barium enema; lateral view) An apple core sign (also called napkin ring sign; green overlay) is seen in the distal descending colon. It is the result of annular constriction by a colorectal carcinoma.

X-ray abdomen (cross-table lateral view) of an infant with necrotizing enterocolitis Small collections of gas (pneumatosis intestinalis; examples indicated by arrowheads) are visible within the intestinal wall. Free intraperitoneal air has ascended to a nondependent position within the anterior abdomen and has extended into the anterior abdominal wall (green overlay). Red line: nasogastric tube

X-ray neck of a young child (AP view) The upper tracheal air column, which normally appears with a square-shouldered configuration, has assumed a conical shape (green overlay) reminiscent of a church steeple (steeple sign; cf. illustration). The steeple sign indicates subglottic airway narrowing and is most commonly seen in croup. However, it may also appear in other conditions (e.g., bacterial tracheitis, tumors). An accompanying lateral radiograph might additionally show inspiratory overdistention of the hypopharynx.

Oral cavity of a female with fever and odynophagia Multiple white vesicles 1–2 mm in diameter and an erythematous rim can be seen on the uvula, soft palate, and tonsillar pillars. These herpes-like vesicles on the posterior oropharynx are characteristic of herpangina, a Coxsackie virus infection mostly seen in young children.

X-ray chest (frontal view) of a premature infant The lungs are opacified and have a granular appearance. Air bronchograms (green overlay) extend to the lung periphery. An endotracheal tube (red outlines) is well-positioned above the carina. In neonatal respiratory distress syndrome (RDS), surfactant deficiency results in alveolar collapse and noncompliant lungs. Air bronchograms are visible when aerated bronchi and bronchioles are surrounded by acinar atelectasis.

Photomicrograph of a blood smear (supravital staining) A mixture of cells can be seen, some of which (reticulocytes) contain deep blue granular or meshwork-like cytoplasmic RNA inclusions (red overlay). Reticulocytes (example indicated by yellow overlay) are slightly larger and rounder than mature erythrocytes and usually lack their characteristic central pallor. Multiple mature erythrocytes can also be seen. They feature no cytoplasmic RNA inclusions, usually show distinct central pallor, and are less spherical than reticulocytes.

The vertical hemorrhages (arrowheads) underneath this patient's fingernails are most commonly caused by trauma but can also occur as a result of immune complex deposition or microthrombosis in subacute, infective endocarditis, SLE, or rheumatoid arthritis.

Fluoroscopy (voiding cystourethrogram; supine; frontal view) Reflux of contrast (vesicoureteral reflux; green overlay) from the bladder (B) into the right ureter is seen, without dilation of the ureter. The proximal collecting system and kidney are not visible on this image. U: urethra
The scalp has a solitary, ovoid, bright red nodule with irregular texture. This finding is suggestive of superficial hemangioma.

A: Upper trunk
B: Lower trunk
C: Long thoracic nerve
D: Posterior cord
E: Axillary nerve
F: Radial nerve
G: Musculocutaneous nerve
H: Median nerve
I: Ulnar nerve

12-lead ECG (paper speed 25 mm/s) – Heart rate: ∼ 115/min
– Irregularly irregular rhythm (irregularly irregular RR intervals)
– Normal axis (R > S in both I and aVF)
– Normal narrow QRS complexes
– No discernable P waves
– Fibrillatory waves (f waves) are visible in all leads.
– No clear isoelectric baseline An irregularly irregular rhythm with no discernable P waves is characteristic of atrial fibrillation. Fibrillatory waves reflect the rapid, disorganized atrial activity underlying this condition, but are not always seen on ECG. Commonly, there is also a rapid ventricular response resulting in tachycardia.

X-ray chest (PA view) of a female patient with a history of atypical pneumonia Opacities are distributed bilaterally, predominantly in the upper lobes. Some of the opacities appear linear (examples indicated by red overlay), while others appear nodular (examples indicated by white arrowheads). Areas of confluent opacification (example indicated by green overlay) are also seen. A lateral radiograph would be helpful to better assess the lower zones, which are partially obscured by the breast shadows. The radiographic appearance is compatible with the history of atypical pneumonia. However, the upper lobe-predominant distribution of opacities would raise the question of other acute and chronic diseases, among them tuberculosis, sarcoidosis, and silicosis.

X-ray chest (PA and lateral views) of a young child A well-defined disc-shaped metallic foreign body is seen behind the trachea in the upper esophagus. On close inspection, a double rim (dashed lines), or halo (green overlay), is visible on the PA view, which indicates that it is a button battery. A step-off (arrows) to the small diameter anode (hatched green overlay) is seen on the lateral view. Recognition of a button battery in the esophagus is a pediatric emergency. Radiographs can frequently differentiate button batteries from similarly sized coins and ascertain the direction of the anode, where the most severe damage from the battery occurs. The anode step-off (arrows) may not be visible on some very thin batteries.

Coronary angiography (left coronary artery; 30-degree right anterior oblique (RAO), 30-degree cranial view) High-grade stenosis (green arrow) of the proximal LAD is seen in the region of a mural plaque (green overlay). LCA: left coronary artery, LAD: left anterior descending artery; LCx: left circumflex artery

CT abdomen (with contrast; coronal plane) Both kidneys are markedly enlarged and feature multiple round-to-ovoid, hypodense lesions of various sizes (examples indicated by green overlay) compatible with cysts. Some smaller cysts are present in the liver (examples indicated by red overlay). Contrast-enhanced renal parenchyma is seen between the cysts (yellow overlay). The CT features are consistent with autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD). B: bladder; L: liver; SB: small bowel
CT head (without contrast; axial plane) Hyperdensity (green overlay) in the basal cisterns indicates the presence of subarachnoid hemorrhage.
Multiple dark-colored pedunculated papules are visible.

MRI Head (T1 weighted; with contrast; coronal plane) There is a well-circumscribed, homogeneously enhancing rounded mass (green overlay) continuous with the falx cerebri and dura of the right parietal bone. It does not invade the surrounding brain tissue or bone. This is a case of meningioma. This is the most common diagnosis for a dural-based mass with smooth margins and homogeneous contrast enhancement. Other important differentials include lymphoma and metastasis, both of which would more likely (but not always) be less uniform in shape and enhanceheterogeneously.

Ultrasound liver of a patient with hepatocellular carcinoma The surface of the liver is irregular (green line) as a result of cirrhosis. A solitary liver lesion demonstrates an isoechoic center (green hatched overlay) and hypoechoic periphery (green overlay). Such an appearance, which has been termed the “halo sign” (also “target sign” or “bull's eye sign”), may indicate a malignant liver lesion. Hepatocellular carcinoma has variable presentations on ultrasound.

ECG strip (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Regular P waves at a rate of ∼80/min (green overlay)
– There is progressive lengthening of the PR interval until a P wave is not conducted (in this example, the fourth, which is not followed by a QRS complex) and the progression restarts.
Erythematous, confluent plaques typical of urticaria that cover most of the skin on the antecubital fossa and the ventral side of the forearm.

CT chest (high resolution; axial plane; lung window) of a patient with usual interstitial pneumonia A diffuse reticular interstitial pattern is accompanied by traction bronchiectasis (examples indicated by green outline) and clustered small cystic air spaces (honeycombing). Multilayered honeycombing (example indicated by red outline) is conspicuous in the periphery of the lungs.

X-ray chest (AP view) of an infant A homogeneous soft tissue opacity representing normal thymic tissue extends laterally from the right side of the superior mediastinum into the right upper hemithorax. Its lateral border is convex while its inferior border is straight (green overlay) producing a sail-like appearance (illustration) that has been termed the thymic sail sign.

Left submammary region of an female older adult An extensive, well-defined erythema is seen. The lesion features multiple macules in its periphery (plus some macules surrounding the lesion). This is the typical appearance of intertrigo.

T1-weighted contrast-enhanced MRI, frontal view: bihemisphere garland-like contrast enhancement. There is peripheral contrast agent enhancement (blue overlay) with central necrosis (green overlay). The third ventricle (arrowhead) is centrally located. The tumor is displacing the posterior horn of the lateral ventricle (red overlay). Diagnosis: bilateral glioblastoma (butterfly glioma).

Photomicrograph of Schistosoma mansoni egg (high magnification) The Schistosoma mansoni egg has the typical oval shape with a tapered anterior end and the characteristic lateral spine near the posterior end.

There is a cluster of vesicles and papules with surrounding erythema. Different lesions occurring simultaneously (called “synchronous polymorphism”) are typical of dermatitis herpetiformis, which is often associated with severe pruritus. Dermatitis herpetiformis typically shows a symmetrical distribution.

X-ray hands (PA view) There is generalized periarticular osteopenia (example indicated by green overlay) and narrowing of several metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joint spaces (example indicated by green line). The marked deformity of the wrists is the result of numerous carpal, base of metacarpal, and radiocarpal erosions (red lines). Erosive scalloping (example indicated by yellow line) also involves the radioulnar joint. The radiographic findings are typical of rheumatoid arthritis with advanced wrist involvement.

Pure tone audiogram The diagram shows a difference of 30dB between air and bone conduction in the right ear versus the left ear. These findings are indicative of middle or external ear pathologies, such as otitis media or tympanic membrane perforation.

Photomicrograph of a bone marrow aspirate smear (Giemsa stain, high magnification) The bone marrow appears hypercellular. Giant mature megakaryocytes, identifiable by their large size and multilobulated nucleus (example indicated by arrowhead), can be seen forming multiple clusters (example indicated by yellow overlay). These are the typical features of essential thrombocythemia (essential thrombocytosis), a myeloproliferative neoplasm.

Photomicrograph of a lymph node tissue specimen (H&E stain, low magnification) The cortex of a lymph node consists of numerous round lymph follicles (examples indicated by green overlay), in whose pale germinal centers (example indicated by arrow) B cells differentiate. The interfollicular areas are rich in T cells. The light areas represent the lymph sinuses (examples indicated by yellow overlay), which are rich in histiocytes. Click on the microscope icon to view the entire specimen through a virtual microscope.

Sample of synovial fluid under polarizing light microscopy Monosodium urate crystals are needle-shaped and appear yellow when their optical axis is oriented parallel to the polarizer. If their axis is perpendicular to the polarizer, they appear blue. The underlying physical principle is called negative birefringence. Detection of negatively birefringent, needle-shaped crystals in synovial fluid confirms the diagnosis of gout.

MRI of the neck and base of the skull (left: T1-weighted, with contrast, sagittal plane; right: T2-weighted, sagittal plane) There is a well-defined mass in the anterior aspect of the foramen magnum, which enhances on the T1-weighted post-contrast image (red overlay) and appears hyperintense to gray matter on the T2-weighted image (green overlay). The mass is continuous with the adjacent thickened and enhancing dura (arrowhead). This is known as the “dural tail sign.” The dural tail sign suggests lesion originates from the dura mater. The sign is most commonly seen in meningiomas but may also be seen in other malignancies originating in the dura mater, such as lymphoma and metastasis.

X-ray chest (AP view; semierect) of a patient with clinical presentation of pneumonia Ill-defined air space opacification is seen in the lower right lung (arrows). Small portions of the right hemidiaphragm are obscured, which suggests that the right lower lobe is involved. Close proximity to the right heart border also raises the question of right middle lobe (medial segment) involvement. PA and lateral views could help distinguish lower and middle lobe involvement. The radiographic findings favor aspiration as the cause of pneumonia. The lower lobe of the right lung is the most common site of aspiration pneumonia in an erect patient due to the vertical orientation of the right main bronchus and its larger caliber compared to the left main bronchus.

Chest x-ray (PA view) of a patient with sarcoidosis The pulmonary hila (green overlay) are enlarged and lobulated. Additionally, the right superior mediastinum (red overlay) is widened and dense. A normal thin right paratracheal stripe is not seen. Relatively symmetric bilateral hilar and right paratracheal lymph node enlargement on a chest radiograph in sarcoidosis is termed the “1-2-3 sign” or “Garland triad.” Examples of other causes of hilar and mediastinal lymphadenopathy include lymphoma, metastatic disease, granulomatous infections, and inorganic dust disease.

Upper GI series (single contrast; lateral view) Narrowing of the second (descending) portion of the duodenum (white arrow) remained fixed during fluoroscopy. A CT scan (not shown) revealed an annular pancreas as the cause. DB: duodenal bulb; S: stomach

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Regular sinus rhythm
– Rate 68/min
– Normal axis (R > S in leads I and aVF)
– Short PR interval (112 ms)
– Wide QRS complexes (126 ms) with delta waves (the slurred initial portion of the QRS complex; zoom box) A short PR interval and widened QRS complexes with delta waves are the characteristic ECG findings of Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome. If seen in asymptomatic patients with no evidence of arrhythmia, this constellation of findings is referred to as the "WPW pattern”.

Single lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Rate: 192/min
– Regular rhythm
– No P or T waves are discernible because of the high ventricular rate.
– QRS complexes are broad (> 120 ms) and monomorphic (there is a similar QRS morphology in all beats). These features are characteristic of monomorphic ventricular tachycardia (VT). VT persisting for ≥ 30 seconds is considered sustained VT (this strip does not show the full 30-second interval).

B: left carotid artery

Photomicrograph of lung tissue (H&E stain, high magnification) An aggregate of basophilic bacteria with radially branching filaments is visible at the center, which has a cauliflower-like appearance (yellow overlay).
The bacteria are surrounded by numerous granulocytes, resulting in a lesion with a rosette-like pattern (green overlay). This is the typical histopathological appearance of a yellow “sulfur granule” caused by actinomycosis.

CT abdomen (without contrast; axial plane) This axial section through the upper abdomen shows no abnormalities. A: stomach; B: liver; C: right psoas major muscle; D: abdominal aorta; E: left kidney; F: spleen

Photomicrograph of a liver biopsy (PAS diastase stain, approx. 450x) There are PAS-positive and diastase-resistant globular cytoplasmic inclusions within periportal hepatocytes (green overlay).
For orientation, the periportal fibrous tissue has been highlighted with a white overlay. These are the characteristic histopathological findings in alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency. However, additional immunochemical staining is needed for confirmation.

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) - Rate ∼60/min
- Regular sinus rhythm
- Normal cardiac axis (R >S in I and aVF)
- Normal PR interval (∼140ms) and QTc (∼400ms)
- Narrow QRS complexes
- Diffuse concave ST elevation (in I, II, aVF, V3–V6) with reciprocal ST depression in aVR and V1 (red overlay). These findings are consistent with acute pericarditis.
Abdomen and groin of a young girl showing grouped, skin-colored papules with central umbilication.

Macroscopic specimen of an ovary, transverse section: There are multiple, circularly arranged, brown subcapsular cysts (examples indicated by green overlay) on the subcapsular region, which are relatively small and of approx. the same size. This is the typical appearance of a polycystic ovary (see illustration for a schematic representation). In addition to the abnormal changes observed here, the ovary is frequently enlarged and as a result there is an increase in stromal tissue.

Barium swallow (lateral view) The sequence of images (left: early; right: late) shows contrast (green overlay) filling a pouch arising from the posterior hypopharynx. The location of the pouch is consistent with a Zenker diverticulum, a posterior outpouching of the hypopharynx just proximal to the upper esophageal sphincter. The diverticulum occurs through a weakness in the muscular wall of the pharynx called the Killian dehiscence.

A: Hippocampus
B: Internal capsule
C: Insula
D: Inferior frontal gyrus
E: Superior temporal gyrus

X-ray abdomen (AP view; erect) Multiple distended bowel loops (examples indicated by white dashed outlines) are seen, many of which contain air-fluid levels (examples indicated by green overlay). A distended colon (with haustra) is not visible. The pattern of the distended loops suggests small bowel obstruction. S: stomach

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Irregularly irregular rhythm – Average ventricular rate is ∼ 144/min (24 QRS complexes can be seen on the 10-second rhythm strip) – No discernable P waves – Narrow QRS complexes – Normal cardiac axis
These findings are characteristic of atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response.

Phase contrast photomicrograph of vaginal discharge (high magnification) These parasites with flagella (blue overlay) are Trichomonas vaginalis, the causative pathogen of trichomonal vaginitis. In the center, two squamous epithelial cells can be seen.

Fundus photography of right (R) and left (L) optic disc of the same patient The optic discs show severely blurred margins (dashed circle indicates supposed margin). The retinal veins are dilated and tortuous (examples marked by white lines). There are multiple peripapillary hemorrhages, which appear as flame-shaped hemorrhages (examples marked by blue overlay). Cotton wool spots are also present and appear as bright spots (examples marked by arrowheads). These features are characteristic of papilledema. If present bilaterally, possible causes include cranial hypertension or hypertensive emergency.

Photomicrograph of a colon biopsy specimen (H&E stain; high magnification) Infiltrating neutrophils (examples indicated by arrowheads) can be seen in the crypts in the upper part of the image. A noncaseating granuloma (green overlay) is visible in the lower part of the image. These features indicate transmural inflammation, which is a characteristic finding of Crohn disease.
Multiple well-demarcated cherry-red papules are distributed on the skin. This appearance is characteristic of cherry angiomas.
Slit-lamp photography of the cornea (fluorescein stain) Dendritic lesions (red overlay) are visible on the cornea. This finding is characteristic of herpes simplex keratitis.
Macroscopic photograph of a colectomy specimen, showing a large number of small polyps throughout the visible section of the colon.

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s)
– Atrial rate ∼ 240/min, ventricular rate ∼ 60/min (4:1 AV conduction ratio)
– Left axis deviation: R > S in I (green complex); S > R in aVF (red complex)
– P waves with a “sawtooth” appearance (flutter waves)
– Regular, narrow QRS complexes
– Prolonged PR interval (∼ 280 ms)
– Horizontal ST depression (0.1–0.2 mV) with T-wave inversion in leads I and V2–V6, suggestive of myocardial ischemia These findings indicate atrial flutter with 4:1 conduction, probably secondary to the observed myocardial ischemia.

Trunk of a male patient showing a plaque and maculae with scaling. There is a plaque with collarettes (primary plaque) beneath the right costal arch that is approx. 3 cm in diameter and small, confluent, homogeneous, oval lesions scattered on the trunk that are partially scaly.

Chest x-ray (PA view) The lungs have a pattern of innumerable small nodular opacities. The hila are enlarged and lobulated (red overlay) and the right paratracheal stripe is widened (green overlay). Bilateral hilar and right mediastinal adenopathy is suggestive of sarcoidosis and is referred to as the “Garland triad” or “1-2-3 sign.”
Well-circumscribed, pale papules (wheals) on an erythematous base (flare) can be seen on this patient's back.

Photomicrograph of a cerebellar tissue specimen (HPS stain; high magnification) Numerous polygonal neoplastic stromal cells with lipid-laden vacuoles (examples indicated by arrowheads) can be seen, resulting in the typical clear-cell appearance. Additionally, there is an abundance of small, thin-walled blood vessels, many of which are densely packed together (examples indicated by blue overlay).

Photomicrograph of a urine sample (urine sediment wet mount, intermediate magnification) Multiple envelope-shaped crystals can be seen (cf. illustration; examples indicated by yellow overlay) throughout the sample. These are composed of calcium oxalate dihydrate and have an octahedral structure.

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Regular rhythm; heart rate ∼190/min.
– Normal cardiac axis (R > S in leads I and aVF)
– Narrow QRS complexes (< 100 ms)
– No visible P waves (they are buried in the QRS complexes)
– Widespread ST segment depression, most prominent in V3–V5 (likely rate-related; indicated by red overlay)
– QRS alternans: QRS amplitude is normal throughout but oscillates (indicated by green line) A regular, narrow-complex tachycardia without visible P-waves is characteristic of AVNRT. Rate-related ST depression and QRS alternans are also commonly observed.

Competitive antagonists are substances that decrease the potency of an agonist without altering its efficacy. The curve on the left illustrates the potency of the receptor agonist alone (A). The curve on the right illustrates the effect of a competitive antagonist (B) on a receptor agonist. In the presence of a competitive agonist (A+B), a higher dose of the receptor agonist is required to achieve the effect of the agonist.
Lacelike, white lesions on the oral mucous membrane that cannot be scraped off (Wickham striae) are visible. This appearance is typical for oral lichen planus.

A well-demarcated area of necrotic skin (eschar) on the dorsum of the thigh. The underlying skin is hyperemic and friable. In a patient who recently received warfarin, such findings are characteristic of warfarin-induced skin necrosis.

Cranial MRI (T1-weighted, with contrast) A hyperintense, round mass with clearly defined borders (green overlay) is seen in the sella turcica (directly above the nasal pharynx). These are typical radiological features of a pituitary adenoma.

ECG strip (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Regular P waves at a rate of ∼80/min (green overlay)
– There is progressive lengthening of the PR interval until a P wave is not conducted (in this example, the fourth, which is not followed by a QRS complex) and the progression restarts.

X: This graph represents the enzyme kinetics of a given enzyme alone (i.e., without drug interference). A: Linear graphs are never seen on Michaelis-Menten plots; a maximum reaction velocity will always be reached because there is only a limited number of enzymes. (Linear graphs are seen on Lineweaver-Burke plots, though, since they are double reciprocal plots.)
B: This graph represents the effect of an allosteric activator (higher affinity).
C: This graph represents an increase in enzyme concentration (higher maximum reaction velocity) plus the effect of a competitive inhibitor (lower affinity).
D: This graph represents the effect of a competitive inhibitor (lower affinity).
A small, solitary, flesh-colored, round, nodular lesion with a small central pore.

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) 0 to 2.2 seconds:
– Sinus rhythm
– Rate: ∼ 55/min
– Axis: normal, between (-30°) and 0° (R > S in leads I and II, R < S in aVF)
– Normal narrow QRS complexes
2.2 to 4 seconds:
– Regular ventricular rhythm with atrioventricular (AV) dissociation (no relationship between P waves and QRS complexes). P waves are mostly obscured due to the high ventricular rate, but can occasionally be seen partially buried in the T wave.
– Rate: ∼ 160/min
– Axis: remains normal but has shifted rightwards, to between 60° and 90° (R > S in lead I, R < S in aVL)
– Wide (∼ 140 ms), monomorphic QRS complexes with LBBB morphology This ECG shows the onset of ventricular tachycardia (VT) following two beats of normal sinus rhythm. Monomorphic VT with LBBB morphology suggests a right ventricular outflow tract (RVOT) origin.
Confluent erythematous plaques with silver-white scaling are visible on the extensor surface of the upper limb.
A nonconfluent, pink, maculopapular rash is visible across this infant's trunk and extremities.
X-ray pelvis (anteroposterior view) Increased density of the femoral heads (green overlay) is accompanied by articular surface irregularity and subchondral lucency (crescent sign; red line overlay).

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Sinus rhythm with a heart rate of ∼ 85/min
– Left axis deviation (R > S in I, S > R in aVF)
– There are both ventricular (green overlay) and supraventricular (purple overlay) extrasystoles.
– ST elevation in I, aVL, and the precordial leads (V1–V6; most markedly in V2–V4), with reciprocal ST depression in III (red overlay)
– Pathological Q waves in V1–V3 (blue overlay)
X-ray neck (lateral view) of a patient with a retropharyngeal abscess Retropharyngeal soft tissue swelling has produced widening (indicated by left-right arrows) of the prevertebral soft tissues.

X-ray of the right shoulder (AP view) The acromiohumeral interval (red overlay; white arrowheads), which is normally ∼10 mm wide, has been obliterated. As some of the rotator cuff tendons lie within the acromiohumeral interval, its obliteration indicates either a tear or degeneration of the rotator cuff tendon(s). MRI is needed to confirm the diagnosis. Int-Rot: internal rotation

Photograph of the upper left arm of a male adult There is an ovoid, yellow-green and, in some regions, red discoloration of the skin on the left upper arm. This appearance is characteristic of a hematoma due to blunt trauma. The yellow-green color indicates that the trauma occurred a few days ago.

Photomicrograph of tendinous tissue (H&E stain; high magnification) There are lipid-laden foam cells (histiocytes; examples indicated by arrowheads) and large cholesterol clefts (examples indicated by green overlay) throughout the tissue. These are typical histological findings in xanthoma.

Fundus photography of the right eye There is massive neovascularization of the optic disk (NVD; examples indicated by white arrows), cotton wool spots (blue overlay), hard exudates (circle), intraretinal hemorrhages (examples indicated by white arrowheads), and a microaneurysm (example indicated by black arrowhead). The diagnosis of proliferative disease requires the presence of neovascularization.

X-ray forefoot (dorsoplantar view) of a patient with diabetes mellitus There is marked deformity of the second metatarsal head and malalignment at the second and third metatarsal-phalangeal (MTP; green circle) and interphalangeal (IP) joints. The proximal phalanges show lateral subluxation. Additional findings include metatarsus varus and hallux valgus with bunion formation (arrow).

X-ray chest (PA view) A thin white line represents the visceral pleura of the left lung (dotted line). No lung markings are seen peripheral to the visceral pleura (green overlay). The appearance is consistent with a left pneumothorax. There is no evidence of contralateral mediastinal shift, diaphragmatic depression, or ipsilateral intercostal space widening to suggest tension pneumothorax.

Photomicrograph of a stool sample (wet mount; iodine stain) An oval-shaped cyst with a prominent, double-layered wall is visible in the center of the image. The cytoplasm contains four nuclei, which are identifiable as spherical structures. It also contains an axostyle, a fibrillary structure from which the flagella of the trophozoite arises. This is the typical appearance of a Giardia lamblia cyst, which is excreted in feces and responsible for the transmission of giardiasis through the ingestion of contaminated food or water.

Chest x-ray (PA view) Large lung volumes (outer green line and arrows) are accompanied by intercostal space widening (examples indicated by red arrows) and diaphragmatic flattening indicative of hyperinflation. Opacification over the lower zones of both hemithoracies is the result of breast tissue.

Ankle of a patient with cutaneous larva migrans Serpiginous marks (larval tracks) are visible here. However, in the case of a strong local inflammatory response, these marks may not be visible. Serpiginous marks together with pruritus establish the diagnosis of cutaneous larva migrans.

The right breast is swollen and shows diffuse erythema that extends over most of its surface. Conspicuous hair follicles produce the typical orange peel appearance (peau d'orange). The nipple is also markedly retracted. Edematous swelling, orange peel appearance, and nipple retraction are all caused by blockage of lymphatic channels. The swelling and erythema distinguish inflammatory breast cancer from locally advanced breast cancer.

X-ray chest (lateral view) of a patient with pericardial constriction Pericardial thickening (green overlay) with calcification (indicated by arrow) is seen. Pleural effusions (yellow overlay) obscure the costophrenic angles. An additional finding in this patient is opacification of the lower lungs by pulmonary edema (blue overlay). Fissural thickening (indicated by red lines) is visible.

Photomicrograph of a peripheral blood smear Most of the red blood cells are small (microcytic) and contain a central zone of pallor (hypochromasia; examples indicated by blue overlay). There are also cells with abnormal morphology (poikilocytosis; examples indicated by arrow-heads). In the center of the image, a distinctly basophilic lymphocyte (example indicated by yellow overlay) is visible, which can be used to compare cell sizes. These findings are characteristic of iron deficiency anemia.
Barium swallow Helical contractions of the lower esophagus (green overlay), resulting in a corkscrew-like appearance. Seen in patients with diffuse esophageal spasm (DES).

A 3–5 cm confluent, white plaque with a hairy appearance is seen across the left lateral side of the tongue that, in this case, could not be scraped off using a spatula. This clinical finding is characteristic of oral hairy leukoplakia.

This 9-month-old infant suffered from a high fever of up to 39.8°C (103.6°F) for 3 days. Following a sudden decrease in temperature, the infant developed a rapidly spreading, patchy, macular exanthem on the neck, behind the ears, and on the entire trunk. The rash lasted for 2 days. The names of the disease reflect its two phases: three-day fever and exanthem subitum (i.e., sudden exanthem).

The presence of multiple poorly marginated sclerotic lesions in Paget disease has been termed “cotton wool appearance.”

This pedigree is consistent with the X-linked recessive inheritance pattern seen in G6PD deficiency. There is no male-to-male transmission. The mother of the patient indicated (red arrow) has a 50% chance of being a carrier, and the patient therefore has a 25% chance of inheriting the disease (0.5 x 0.5 = 0.25).
The overlay shows the carrier status of the women.

Photomicrograph of a peripheral blood smear (Wright-Giemsa stain; high magnification): An increased density of small lymphocytic cells (examples indicated by blue arrowheads) can be seen, indicating leukocytosis in chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Some neoplastic lymphocytes appear artificially ruptured (examples indicated by red arrowheads). These are referred to as “smudge cells” or “Gumprecht shadows.” Smudge cells are a characteristic feature of chronic lymphocytic leukemia, albeit not a specific sign.

Photomicrograph of a maxillary sinus biopsy specimen (H&E stain; high magnification) Broad, nonseptate hyphae with right-angle branching are visible (example indicated by green overlay). These are accompanied by numerous neutrophils indicating acute sinusitis. This is the typical appearance of mucormycosis.

X-ray left knee (AP view) of a 23-year-old patient with a giant cell tumor (osteoclastoma) A lucent lesion in the proximal tibia (green overlay) has a bubbly appearance and well-defined nonsclerotic margins. The cortex is scalloped and thinned. The growth plate is closed and the lesion extends to the subarticular bone. The differential diagnosis of bubbly bone lesions includes giant cell tumors. Most giant cell tumors are eccentrically located, although large lesions may appear central, as in this radiograph. Some giant cell tumors contain aneurysmal bone cyst components.

Posteroanterior chest x-ray showing a large amount of intraabdominal free gas (green area) that contours the diaphragm (white dashed line). Free gas in the peritoneal cavity indicates pneumoperitoneum, which is often a sign of critical illness.
