CT chest (with contrast; axial plane; mediastinal window) of a patient with Marfan syndrome A large aneurysm of the ascending aorta (green overlay) is seen. Pectus excavatum deformity (white arrow) is also present and deviates the mediastinum to the left of midline. Aneurysmal dilatation of the ascending aorta and pectus excavatum can both be seen in Marfan syndrome. AA = Ascending aorta; DA = Descending aorta; MPA = Main pulmonary artery

X-ray left knee (lateral view) of patient with history of recurrent minor trauma and chronic proximal anterior tibial pain An ossified fragment (green overlay) is separate from the remainder of the tibial tuberosity, which shows an irregular margin (yellow overlay). There is thickening of the patellar tendon (red overlay) and pretibial soft tissues (blue overlay). In conjunction with the clinical presentation, the radiographic features are consistent with Osgood-Schlatter disease.
Erythema localized to the extensor surface of both legs There are red, nodular skin lesions due to inflammation of the subcutaneous tissue.

Photomicrograph of a liver tissue sample stained for fungi (methenamine silver stain) Multiple large, round yeast cells are present. On some of the cells, narrow-necked budding can be seen, resulting in the characteristic “captain's wheel” formation (illustration; example indicated by green overlay). This is the typical appearance of the budding yeasts causing paracoccidioidomycosis.

ERCP (positive mode, ventral view) Multifocal strictures with dilation and beading of the intrahepatic bile ducts are visible (examples indicated by green outlines). These findings are characteristic of primary sclerosing cholangitis.

Photomicrograph of an unstained wet stool mount A thin-shelled, oval egg (dashed borderline) with segmented morphology (due to multiple blastomeres formed by cell division, yellow overlay) is seen. This is the typical appearance of Ancylostoma and Necator eggs.
Photograph of a nephrectomy specimen A large, yellow mass (green overlay) is visible infiltrating the top half of the kidney (K).

Sample of synovial fluid under polarizing light microscopy Monosodium urate crystals are needle-shaped and appear yellow when their optical axis is oriented parallel to the polarizer. If their axis is perpendicular to the polarizer, they appear blue. The underlying physical principle is called negative birefringence. Detection of negatively birefringent, needle-shaped crystals in synovial fluid confirms the diagnosis of gout.

Lead I from a 12 lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) - Irregularly irregular rhythm (irregularly irregular R-R intervals; examples indicated by red overlay) with a ventricular rate of approx. 60/min
- No visible P waves
- Narrow QRS complexes (< 120 ms)
- Coarse fibrillatory waves (f waves; examples indicated by green overlay) can be seen between the QRS complexes. These are characterized by low amplitude, high frequency, and irregular morphology and should not be confused with P waves or flutter waves. This appearance is typical of atrial fibrillation.

X-ray right clavicle (AP view) of a child after a recent fall on an outstretched arm A subtle nondisplaced fracture (green overlay) is seen at the mid to medial third of the right clavicle. There is no callus formation. Arrowheads: overlying artefact; red overlay: growth plate
Photomicrograph of a bacterial culture (Gram stain; high magnification) E. coli is a rod-shaped and gram-negative bacterium.
Scale in cm Black pigment stones are a byproduct of hemolysis. They are composed of calcium bilirubinate.

Nuclear medicine thyroid scan (technetium-99m pertechnetate radioisotope; anterior and lateral views) Increased uptake of radiotracer is visible in the hyoid region and lingual or sublingual regions. There is no uptake in the normal location of the thyroid gland in the anterior neck. The findings indicate two sites of ectopic thyroid tissue. The possibility of ectopic tissue in a thyroglossal duct cyst would also be a consideration for the hyoid region uptake.
Multiple vesicles with an erythematous base are visible on the finger. These findings are suggestive of dyshidrotic eczema.
Erythematosquamous plaques with silver-white scaling and surrounding erythema are visible over the extensor surface of the knee.

X-ray chest (lateral decubitus view; A: right lateral decubitus, B: left lateral decubitus) of a child who aspirated an almond No radiopacity demonstrating the foreign body is seen. However, the right lung (red outlines) remains hyperinflated when the patient is positioned with the right side down on the radiographic table. The hyperinflation reflects air trapping from foreign body aspiration into the right main bronchus. Decubitus radiographs are sometimes used to evaluate for the presence of an inhaled foreign body. The expectation is that a dependent lung will partially collapse in a normal patient, but will remain hyperinflated in the setting of an obstructive foreign body. Green outlines: left lung

Photomicrograph of blood cells from a bone marrow sample (Prussian blue stain) Two immature RBCs can be seen (green overlay). They feature round basophilic inclusions (iron deposits) within their mitochondria (red overlay). Most of the mitochondria surround the nucleus (yellow overlay).
Such cells are referred to as sideroblasts.
Sideroblasts are seen in diseases such as sideroblastic anemia, hemolytic anemia, and sickle cell disease.

MRI cervical spine (T2-weighted; sagittal plane) Cervical disk herniation at C6–C7 (green overlay) produces a prominent anterior extradural impression with spinal canal narrowing and cord compression.

CT abdomen (coronal plane) Marked segmental thickening of the wall of the sigmoid colon (green overlay) is seen in the region of diverticula (examples indicated by red overlay). There is perisigmoid inflammatory stranding (yellow overlay) adjacent to a small gas collection (blue overlay). The gas could be within a broad-based diverticulum or a cluster of diverticula. Alternatively, it could be extraluminal and indicative of perforation. Oral contrast is present within the sigmoid lumen and within multiple small bowel loops (SB). The imaging findings are compatible with sigmoid diverticulitis. B: bladder; DC: descending colon

An asymmetric, dark brown plaque with irregular borders is visible. Within the lesion, an amelanotic, pinkish nodule can be seen. This appearance is suggestive of a superficial spreading melanoma with a nodular component.

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) - Sinus rhythm at a rate of approx. 55/min with an atrial premature beat (green overlay) after every two sinus beats (blue overlay); this is referred to as “trigeminy.” - The ectopic P waves coincide with the preceding T-wave to produce a larger, notched wave (yellow overlay; best seen in rhythm strip II) - QRS complexes are widened (duration > 120 ms) for both sinus and premature beats. There is aberrant conduction of the premature beats, with a right bundle branch block morphology: rSR′ in V1–V3 (example indicated by red overlay), with a wide, slurred S wave in the lateral leads. - There is no full compensatory pause following the premature beat (RR interval between the preceding and the following sinus QRS complexes is less than twice the normal RR interval)

Photomicrograph of a hepatic nodule biopsy specimen (H&E stain) Numerous cystic spaces lined by a single layer of endothelial cells are visible throughout this microscopic field (examples indicated by green overlay). The cyst walls are thin and composed of smooth muscle, elastic tissue, and fibrous tissue. Multiple thrombi (examples indicated by arrowheads) are visible within the cysts. This histopathological appearance is characteristic of cavernous liver hemangioma.
A ∼5 cm, blue-gray macula with irregular borders is visible on the back. The macula has no evidence of hypertrichosis.

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Rhythm and rate (best seen in lead II rhythm strip): three sinus beats (rate ∼ 35/min), followed by a sinus pause of ∼ 3.8 s, after which a junctional escape rhythm takes over (QRS complex identical to sinus beats, but no P wave visible)
– Normal cardiac axis (leads I and II positive, lead aVF isoelectric)
– Wide QRS complexes (∼ 140 ms)
– Normal PR interval (∼ 180 ms) and corrected QT interval (∼ 430 ms)
Photograph of a lung pathology specimen A large solid mass (green overlay) with central necrosis (red overlay) is visible within the upper lobe of the lung.
A round, well demarcated erythematous lesion with central scaling is visible. The border is raised with several pustules visible. These features are characteristic of tinea corporis.

Erythematous swelling on the proximal interphalangeal joint of the fifth digit and on the interphalangeal joint of the thumb. Nail involvement (nail dystrophy with an oil drop sign and pitted nails) is also evident.

Coronal section of a formalin-fixed brain The tumor presents itself as a gray-red, partly necrotic area of the left-temporal and temporal lobe (green overlay). The tumor has also spread into the corpus callosum (C).

Upper ankle region with a round, red-brown, scaly plaque. Nummular eczema is usually associated with severe pruritus. Tinea corporis should be considered as a differential diagnosis.
Lines of fine scaling, forming a network in between dry and coarse skin (xerosis cutis), are visible on this patient's shins and feet.

Photomicrograph of cerebellar vermis tissue (H&E stain) Numerous small, blue, round cells with scant cytoplasm can be seen throughout the specimen (examples indicated by green overlay). Their nuclei vary in size and structure (nuclear pleomorphism) and many cells feature distinctly hyperchromatic nuclei.
The majority of the cells are arranged in concentric clusters around lighter central areas consisting of neuropil (examples indicated by yellow overlay). These clusters are referred to as Homer Wright rosettes. These histopathological features, particularly the Homer Wright rosettes, are characteristic of a medulloblastoma.

Infant's left foot The second digit is constricted, presumably by a hair. The entire digit is markedly swollen and the distal part is also reddened (due to blood being trapped). This condition requires swift removal of the hair to prevent irreversible damage.

Photomicrograph of a lung biopsy (H&E stain, medium-power magnification) Poorly formed noncaseating (nonnecrotizing) granulomas are visible. The largest granuloma (arrow) has a core of large, pale epithelioid macrophages surrounded by lymphocytes and an outer layer of fibrous tissue. Smaller granulomas, multinucleated giant cells (blue overlay), and foamy histiocytes (examples indicated by arrowheads) are also visible. There is a dense chronic inflammatory infiltrate of lymphocytes and plasma cells in the pulmonary interstitium. These histological findings, in combination with a patient history of antigen-related symptoms, are suggestive of hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
Soles of a patient with acute unilateral limb ischemia. Marked purplish discoloration (cyanosis) of the right foot can be seen (likely caused by thromboembolism of the posterior tibial artery).

Photomicrograph of a testicle biopsy specimen (H&E stain, very high magnification) Lymphocytic infiltrates are clearly visible (examples indicated by yellow overlay). Tumor cells appear with a central nucleus with nucleolus and clear cytoplasm (examples indicated by green overlay). These features are suggestive of seminoma.

Osteosarcoma (green overlay) of the distal femur with dorsal perforation of the cortical bone (red dashed line) and filling of the medullary cavity, the epiphysis, and the metaphysis. A hemorrhagic tumor mass is seen in the dorsal soft tissue (red overlay).

– There are both ventricular (green overlay) and supraventricular (purple overlay) extrasystoles.

– Pathological Q waves in V1–V3 (blue overlay)

An erythematous rash with numerous small vesicles in a linear pattern can be seen on the left forearm. This rash is typical of mild allergic contact dermatitis caused by urushiol-containing plants (e.g., poison oak). In more severe cases, bullae and pronounced edema may be present.

Multiple pustules and erosions are visible on this neonate's trunk.

X-ray chest (PA view) of a patient with bronchogenic carcinoma A solitary nodule (red overlay) with an irregular margin is visible in the periphery of the upper left lung. An additional soft-tissue opacity peripheral to the nodule along the ribs may represent adjacent pleural thickening (green overlay). The differential diagnosis for a solitary pulmonary nodule includes neoplastic (malignant and benign), inflammatory, and congenital conditions.

Barium swallow Smooth funnel-shaped tapering (green overlay) in the region of the lower esophageal sphincter is accompanied by widening (arrow) of the prestenotic distal esophagus (red overlay). The appearance of the esophagus in achalasia has been referred to as the “bird-beak sign” (illustration). If the contour of the narrowed region is irregular and/or involves more than just the distal esophagus, the alternative diagnosis of esophageal carcinoma (pseudoachalasia) must be considered.

Photomicrograph of a peripheral blood smear Numerous normal RBCs are visible. One of the cells is filled with dark aggregations of chromatin (green overlay). RBCs with this appearance are referred to as schizonts. Schizonts are found in patients infected with protozoa (e.g., Plasmodium falciparum).
An erythematous, blue-purple area of skin measuring approx. 10 cm x 5 cm is visible over the sacrum with a small area of superficial (epidermal) skin loss near the lower edge.

Photomicrograph of urine sediment (phase-contrast microscopy; high magnification) White blood cell (WBC) casts (blue overlay) contain leukocytes (examples outlined in yellow) that are compressed in a matrix. Sharp margins are characteristic of WBC casts, differentiating them from randomly assembled leukocyte conglomerates.

MRI head (a: fluid attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR); sagittal plane; b: T2-weighted, axial plane) of a patient with multiple sclerosis Multiple hyperintense lesions (examples indicated by green overlay) are seen in the corpus callosum and periventricular white matter.

Photomicrograph of a peripheral blood smear Red blood cells are aggregated in chains and appear like stacked coins (rouleaux formation (illustration); examples indicated by blue overlay). This phenomenon occurs in disorders with increased serum proteins (e.g., multiple myeloma and Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia).

Photomicrograph of a peripheral blood smear Multiple spherocytes can be seen (red circles), which have a typical round (rather than biconcave) shape and no central pallor. The erythrocytes vary significantly in size, which is consistent with anisocytosis (red arrows). On electron microscopy (overlay box with drawings based on EM appearance), the spherical shape of spherocytes (1) and normal biconcave erythrocytes (2) can be seen.

X-ray skull (lateral view) The calvarium is markedly thickened (left right arrow), with widening of the diploic space. Numerous ill-defined lytic (examples indicated by green overlay) and sclerotic (examples indicated by red overlay) lesions are present. The presence of multiple poorly marginated sclerotic lesions in Paget disease has been termed “cotton wool appearance.”
Four ellipsoid, hypopigmented macules (ash-leaf spots) are located on the back of the patient (green overlay). The presence of more than three ash-leaf spots is characteristic of tuberous sclerosis.

Photomicrograph of a lymph node tissue sample (H&E stain, high magnification) Large macrophages with clear cytoplasm (examples indicated by red arrows and green overlays) are interspersed between numerous medium-sized, monomorphic neoplastic lymphocytes. This appearance is known as the starry sky pattern and is a characteristic feature of Burkitt lymphoma. Many of the lymphoma cells show mitotic activity (examples indicated by yellow overlays). A longitudinally sectioned blood vessel is indicated by a black arrowhead.

Photomicrograph of a bone marrow specimen (H&E stain) Plasma cells are large cells that typically have an eccentric nucleus (hatched overlay) with dense, clumped chromatin. The cytoplasm is deeply basophilic due to the abundance of rough endoplasmic reticulum required for the synthesis of antibodies.

CT abdomen (with contrast; axial plane; left: soft tissue window; right: bone window) The calcified wall of the gallbladder is seen as a hyperdense oval outline (green overlay) in the gallbladder fossa. The greater density of the gallbladder calcification compared to the intravenous contrast in several other organs is more conspicuous on the bone window than the soft tissue window. The calcification is diagnostic of a porcelain gallbladder, a rare complication of chronic gallbladder inflammation. L: Liver; IVC: Inferior vena cava; LK: Left kidney; RK: Right kidney; S: Spleen; Arrow: Kidney cyst

The upper blue tracing shows the fetal heart rate (FHR) in beats per minute (bpm), with a baseline of approx. 136 bpm (dashed green line overlay). The FHR baseline lies within the normal range (green shading overlay) and shows moderate variability (∼ 10 bpm; normal range 6–25/minute). The lower blue tracing shows the maternal uterine contractions (in mm Hg). The three decelerations visible are abrupt, with < 30 s from onset to nadir (red arrow overlay), and vary in their shape and their temporal relation to the uterine contractions (dashed red line overlay). These findings are characteristic of variable decelerations. As the decelerations here occur with ≥ 50% of uterine contractions over a 20-minute time period, they are termed “recurrent variable decelerations.” This pattern is concerning for umbilical cord compression/prolapse. If persistent, it may prompt considering emergency cesarean delivery.

There is marked erythema extending from the philtrum to both nasolabial folds, along both sides of the nasal bridge, and on the labiomental groove. There is also minor scaling of the skin on both sides of the nasal bridge. These findings are consistent with seborrheic dermatitis.

X-ray skull (lateral view) of a patient with a history of multiple myeloma Numerous very small, well-circumscribed (punched-out) lytic lesions without sclerotic margins are seen (examples indicated by arrowheads).
A slightly raised, asymmetrical lesion with irregular borders and pigmentation is visible. The diameter is approximately 12 mm. These findings are suggestive of superficial spreading melanoma.

Photomicrograph of cerebral tissue (Gallyas-Braak stain) Intracellular aggregates of hyperphosphorylated tau protein (neurofibrillary tangles) are visible in multiple neurons (arrows indicate examples). Neurofibrillary tangles are a characteristic feature of Alzheimer disease but are also found in other conditions.

Photomicrograph of a myocardial tissue specimen (H&E stain; high magnification) A collagenous nodule (green overlay) is seen within the myocardial tissue. The nodule contains a loose lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate and large eosinophilic cells with an ovoid nucleus (Anitschkow cells, arrowheads). Anitschkow cells are thought to be histiocytes. These nodules, referred to as “Aschoff bodies,” are thought to represent myocardial granulomas, which are typically found in rheumatic heart disease.

X-ray chest (PA view) A single-chamber pacemaker is visible, with the pulse generator (G) on the upper right chest wall and a single lead wire (green dashed line) in the right ventricle. Additional radiographic findings include mild cardiac silhouette enlargement, aortic tortuosity, and thoracic spine curvature with degenerative changes.

Fundus photography of the right eye There is massive neovascularization of the optic disk (NVD; examples indicated by white arrows), cotton wool spots (blue overlay), hard exudates (circle), intraretinal hemorrhages (examples indicated by white arrowheads), and a microaneurysm (example indicated by black arrowhead). The diagnosis of proliferative disease requires the presence of neovascularization.

Four transverse slices of the heart There is concentric hypertrophy (indicated by arrows) of the left ventricular myocardium (green overlay), which has narrowed the lumen (red overlay) and caused a reduction in the intraventricular volume.

Confluent nodular, cauliflower-like, verrucous lesions are visible on the entire labia and the anogenital region. These findings are characteristic of Condylomata acuminata, which are mostly caused by infection with HPV types 11 and 16.

CT chest (high resolution; axial plane; lung window) of a patient with usual interstitial pneumonia A diffuse reticular interstitial pattern is accompanied by traction bronchiectasis (examples indicated by green outline) and clustered small cystic air spaces (honeycombing). Multilayered honeycombing (example indicated by red outline) is conspicuous in the periphery of the lungs.

CT abdomen (with contrast; axial plane) An enlarged heterogeneous pancreatic head (green overlay) contains multiple calcifications, which is characteristic of chronic pancreatitis. Peripancreatic inflammatory fatty stranding (red overlay) is also seen. Incidental findings include a small hypodense cyst (yellow overlay) in the dorsal parenchyma of the left kidney and atherosclerotic vascular calcifications (blue overlay). Intravenous contrast is seen in the liver (L), and oral contrast is seen in the bowel loops (indicated by arrows).
There is bulbous enlargement of the distal phalanges and the nail curvatures are markedly increased.

CT head (with contrast; axial plane) of a patient with HIV Multiple ring-enhancing lesions are accompanied by hypodense perilesional edema (E). Cerebral toxoplasmosis typically presents with multiple lesions, most commonly in the basal ganglia, thalami, and the corticomedullary junction. They appear hypodense without contrast and often show ring enhancement (red overlay) following contrast administration. Rings may be accompanied by eccentric enhancing nodules (example indicated by yellow overlay) resulting in the eccentric (or asymmetric) target sign considered highly suggestive of toxoplasmosis.
A decubitus ulcer with loss of all skin layers and subcutaneous involvement is visible to the right of the intergluteal cleft.

X-ray lumbar spine (AP view) Marginal syndesmophytes formed by ossification of outer fibers of the annulus fibrosis have caused the spine to resemble a bamboo stem (so-called “bamboo spine”; red outlines). Additional ossification of supraspinous and interspinous ligaments has produced a dense line (dagger sign; green overlay) projecting through the vertebrae. Fusion (ankylosis) across the sacroiliac joints (indicated by arrowheads) can also be seen.

Photomicrograph of a urine sample with a pH of 5 (phase contrast microscopy; high magnification) Multiple crystals of varying sizes and shapes can be seen (blue overlay). Their diamond-shaped (rhomboid) form is one of the characteristic appearances of uric acid crystals, which only form in urine samples with a pH < 5.5 and are typically seen in patients with gout or in healthy individuals on a high-purine diet. Uric acid crystals may also appear barrel-shaped and needle-shaped (not seen in this image).

X-ray distal left forearm (left: lateral view; right: PA view) of an adolescent A greenstick fracture of the distal radius is seen. The cortex on the radial (lateral) side of the fracture is disrupted and buckled (green overlay). However, the fracture line has not traversed the bone. The ulnar (medial) side of the radius is bowed, but the cortex is intact (black dotted line). Greenstick fractures are incomplete fractures. Typical locations are the shafts of the bones of the forearm and lower leg. Red overlay: growth plate

Ultrasound right kidney (transhepatic longitudinal view) There is a thin-walled, round, sharply demarcated, anechoic lesion (green overlay) within the renal parenchyma. There is also posterior acoustic enhancement (white overlay). The renal capsule is demarcated by a white line (blue overlay) and the liver is seen adjacent to the kidney (red overlay). These findings are consistent with a renal cyst.

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Irregularly irregular rhythm (examples of the irregular R-R intervals are indicated by red arrows) – Average ventricular rate ∼ 84/min (14 QRS complexes can be seen on the 10-second rhythm strip) – No discernable P waves – Narrow QRS complexes (examples indicated by green lines) – Normal cardiac axis These findings are characteristic of atrial fibrillation.

CT abdomen (without contrast; axial plane) Atrophy and nodularity (indicated by white arrows) are seen along the anterolateral margin of the liver. The spleen is enlarged (green overlay). The findings are consistent with hepatic cirrhosis with portal hypertension and splenomegaly. An additional finding is a small hypoattenuating lesion in the lateral segment of the left lobe.

Photomicrograph of peripheral blood smear Throughout the specimen, there are cells with an irregular, oval form and marked hyperchromasia (intense staining) that are known as macrocytes (red circles). These are surrounded by normal erythrocytes (green circles). Macrocytes may be found in megaloblastic forms of anemia.
Multiple, round lesions with hair loss caused by Microsporum canis.

Photomicrograph of a myometrium nodule specimen (H&E stain; high magnification) Spindle cells in longitudinal (green overlay) and transverse sections can be seen. This intermixed fascicular architecture causes a whorled appearance. The cells match the morphologic criteria of smooth muscle cells (intense eosinophilic cytoplasm and cigar-shaped nuclei (illustration bottom right; examples indicated by arrowheads)) and have no signs of atypia.

Photomicrograph of a blood smear (Giemsa stain; high magnification) An increased number of mature neutrophils (green overlay) with segmented nuclei are present in this blood smear, indicating neutrophilia. These are surrounded by normal erythrocytes (examples indicated by red overlay) and thrombocytes (examples indicated by arrowheads).
X-ray of the left hand (PA view) A distinct radiolucent fracture line (green overlay) is visible across the mid-portion of the scaphoid (white dashed outline).

CT chest (with contrast; axial plane) A large embolism burden is seen (green overlay), with extension across the bifurcation of the pulmonary trunk into the right and left pulmonary arteries. Straddling of the pulmonary trunk bifurcation by pulmonary embolism is sometimes referred to as saddle embolism. AA: ascending aorta; DA: descending aorta; E: esophagus; LMB: left main bronchus; LPA: left pulmonary artery; PT: pulmonary trunk; RMB: right main bronchus; RPA: right pulmonary artery; SVC: superior vena cava

There are bright red cheeks (slapped cheek appearance) and a diffuse, red, confluent maculopapular rash visible over the body of this 16-month-old infant. This is the characteristic cutaneous appearance of fifth disease (erythema infectiosum), caused by infection with parvovirus B19.

Ultrasound pelvis (15 weeks' gestation) Twin pregnancy shows a single placenta. There is no intertwin membrane. The left twin is imaged in the coronal plane. The right twin is imaged in the sagittal plane. A: amniotic fluid; H: head; P: placenta

Fundus photography of the right (R) and left (L) optic disc of the same patient The optic discs have severely blurred margins (dashed circle indicates supposed margin). The retinal veins are dilated and tortuous (examples indicated by white lines). There are multiple peripapillary hemorrhages, which appear as flame-shaped hemorrhages (examples indicated by blue overlay). Cotton wool spots are also present and appear as bright spots (examples indicated by arrowheads). These features are characteristic of papilledema. If present bilaterally, possible causes include cranial hypertension and hypertensive emergency.

Disseminated papules, pustules, and comedones on an erythematous base are visible on the seborrheic skin of the patient's forehead. These findings are consistent with moderate acne vulgaris.

Multiple, painless hard lesions over the left ear helix. Other common sites include the knee, elbow, metatarsal-phalangeal joints, olecranon bursae and tendon insertion points ie achilles tendon. These findings are in keeping of a patient with gout.

The ventral forearm of an adult presenting with a pruritic rash affecting both forearms Multiple purplish-red papules and plaques are visible on the wrist. These papules and plaques are well demarcated, polygonal, and partially confluent. There are, furthermore white lines, known as Wickham striae, on their surface. The localization and appearance of this pruritic rash are typical of lichen planus.

Photomicrograph of a sputum sample An elongated, yellow asbestos (or ferruginous) body can be seen (green overlay). It is surrounded by an iron protein coat and has a characteristic dumbbell shape (illustration).

Photomicrograph of a canine urine sample Sample shows clear, euhedral, rectangular prism crystals (struvite crystals with their characteristic coffin-lid appearance (cf. illustration); examples indicated by green overlay) composed of magnesium, ammonium, and phosphate. In humans, struvite crystals are only seen in upper urinary tract infections caused by urease-producing organisms (e.g., Proteus or Klebsiella species).

Volume-pressure curve of the respiratory apparatus, which illustrates the compliances of the lung and thorax. When added the compliance of the lung and the thorax, form the compliance of the whole respiratory apparatus. The pressure-volume curve relates to passive changes in lung volume when the respiratory muscles are relaxed. While only the graph of the respiratory apparatus seems to be the only one, all the graphs are physiologically S-shaped. A steep slope correlates with high compliance of the tissue. The broken line in the middle of the y-axis illustrates the functional residual capacity.

Photomicrograph of a liver tissue specimen (Giemsa stain; very high magnification) Intracellular (examples indicated by white arrowheads) and extracellular (examples indicated by black arrowheads) forms of ovoid yeast-like organisms are visible. The presence of these organisms is indicative of histoplasmosis. Diagnosis must be confirmed by culture and PCR array to determine the exact species (Histoplasma capsulatum in this case).
There is infestation and destruction of the nasal mucosa with ulcerations extending to the nasal septum and skin.

X-ray chest (PA view) There is increased radiolucency of both lungs with rarefaction of peripheral pulmonary vessels. Due to pulmonary hyperinflation (green outlines and arrows), all twelve ribs are clearly visible. The intercostal spaces are widened (examples indicated by white arrows) and the diaphragm is depressed and flattened (white dashed lines). The left mediastinum appears enlarged (white arrowheads). The small, round, radiopaque objects are most likely buttons of the patient's clothes.

Right big toe of a patient with diabetes mellitus A well-demarcated ulceration is visible on the medial aspect of the foot. This appearance together with a consistent medical history is suggestive of a diabetic foot ulcer, which is likely caused by diabetic polyneuropathy.

ECG strip (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Regular P waves at a rate of ∼80/min (green overlay)
– There is progressive lengthening of the PR interval until a P wave is not conducted (in this example, the fourth, which is not followed by a QRS complex) and the progression restarts.
Left anterior thigh of a young girl Several individual papules with central umbilication can be seen.
X-ray chest (PA view) Free intraperitoneal gas (green overlay) is visible under both the right and left hemidiaphragms.

MRI head (a: T1-weighted; with contrast; sagittal plane; b: T2-weighted; axial plane) of a child A large lesion in the right cerebellar hemisphere displaces the vermis to the left of midline. It has a large cystic component (T1 hypointense and T2 hyperintense; red overlay) and a heterogeneously enhancing solid nodular component (green overlay). Cerebellar pilocytic astrocytoma frequently manifests as a large cystic lesion with an enhancing mural nodule.

Photomicrograph of a renal tissue biopsy specimen (PAS stain, high magnification) An abnormally thickened arteriole is seen near the center of the image. Its wall is thickened due to amorphous deposits of hyaline material (mainly leaked plasma proteins; blue overlay), resulting in luminal narrowing and, consequently, impaired perfusion. These findings are characteristic of hyaline arteriolosclerosis.

A cluster of three lesions is visible on the distal phalanx. The lesions present as nonpurulent vesicles on an erythematous base. The surrounding skin is intact. This is the typical appearance of herpetic whitlow due to herpes simplex virus infection.

Left arm of a child showing multiple, uniform vesicles on an erythematous base. The dermatomal distribution (C8 and T1) is characteristic of shingles. Patients complain of severe pain prior to the appearance of lesions.

Right eye There is local nodular erythematous swelling of the medial part of the right lower eyelid. This finding is consistent with an internal stye, an acute bacterial inflammation of a meibomian gland in the eyelid.

Cranial MRI (T2-weighted; axial view) Bilateral, symmetrical hyperintensities are visible on the medial portions of the thalamus (green overlay). This finding is characteristic of Wernicke encephalopathy.
Scattered, partially coalescent violaceous papules, macules, and plaques are visible on the shins.

A: agonist
B: partial agonist

X-ray chest (PA view) Bilateral heterogeneous consolidations (green overlay) show an upper-lobe-predominant distribution. There are scattered small air space nodules (examples indicated by yellow overlay), and cavities (red overlay) are present within areas of confluent opacification. The leftward tracheal deviation (indicated by black dashed outline and arrow) confirms the presence of left upper lobe volume loss. The left apical cap (blue overlay) could be the result of pleural thickening, fibrosis, extrapleural fat, or a combination of these features. The left costophrenic angle blunting (indicated by the white dashed line) could represent pleural thickening or a small effusion. The radiographic findings shown are common in post-primary tuberculosis.

CT head (axial section; noncontrast) An epidural hematoma is visible as a solitary biconvex hyperdense lesion in the left frontotemporal region (green overlay), causing a midline shift to the contralateral side (indicated by lines and arrows). The soft tissue of the scalp overlying the lesion also appears hyperdense, indicating a scalp hematoma (red overlay).

MRI spine (T2-weighted; sagittal plane) A hyperintense collection (green overlay) in the posterior spinal epidural space at level T11–L1 causes a narrowing of the spinal canal and compression of the spinal cord. There is an increased T2 signal (red overlay) within the cord, likely reflecting cord edema. These findings are consistent with spinal epidural hematoma.
Dark red, regularly demarcated erythema with fine scaling, particularly in the area of the beard; typically associated with strong pruritus.

The upper blue tracing shows the fetal heart rate (FHR) in beats per minute (bpm), with a baseline of approx. 136 bpm (dashed green line overlay). The FHR baseline lies within the normal range (green shading overlay) and shows moderate variability (∼ 10 bpm; normal range 6–25/minute). The lower blue tracing shows the maternal uterine contractions (in mm Hg). The three decelerations visible are abrupt, with < 30 s from onset to nadir (red arrow overlay), and vary in their shape and their temporal relation to the uterine contractions (dashed red line overlay). These findings are characteristic of variable decelerations. As the decelerations here occur with ≥ 50% of uterine contractions over a 20-minute time period, they are termed “recurrent variable decelerations.” This pattern is concerning for umbilical cord compression/prolapse. If persistent, it may prompt considering emergency cesarean delivery.

Peripheral blood smear with normal erythrocytes (E) and schistocytes (dashed circles). One side of the schistocyte retains the typical convex shape of an erythrocyte, while the fragmented side is irregular or concave (green line) and often shows pointed edges. Schistocytes occur due to thrombotic microangiopathies (e.g., due to TTP, DIC, etc.) or mechanical damage (e.g., artificial heart valves).Click on the microscope icon to view the entire specimen through a virtual microscope.

The upper blue tracing shows the fetal heart rate (FHR) in beats per minute (bpm) with a baseline of approx. 130 bpm (green dashed line overlay). The FHR baseline lies within the normal range (green shading overlay) and shows moderate variability (~10 bpm; normal range 6–25 bpm). The lower blue tracing shows the maternal uterine contractions (in mm Hg).
Contractions and decelerations begin and end together (red dashed line overlay), with the peaks of the contractions coinciding with the nadirs of the decelerations (red arrow overlay). The decelerations are gradual, with ≥ 30 s from onset to nadir. These findings are characteristic of early decelerations.
Forearm An ulcer-like lesion with central, black necrosis and sporadic vesicles towards the left margin of the lesion can be seen.

MRI head (left: T2-weighted; right: diffusion-weighted; axial plane) of a patient with common variable immunodeficiency syndrome A confluent, hyperintense area (green overlay) without mass effect is seen in the right frontoparietal subcortical white matter. High signal intensity on the diffusion-weighted image (red overlay) indicates diffusion restriction. The MRI features and immunocompromised state suggest progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML), even though PML lesions are typically multifocal, bilateral, and asymmetric. PML lesions generally show little or no mass effect or contrast enhancement.
There is an ulcer with an erythematous margin on the corona of the glans penis. This is a characteristic chancroid lesion, which is caused by Haemophilus ducreyi infection.

CT abdomen (with contrast; coronal plane) The left renal collecting system (green overlay) is dilated to the level of a hyperdense calculus (yellow overlay) within the proximal third of the ureter. The left renal parenchyma shows less enhancement than the right renal parenchyma as a result of impaired excretion from ureteral obstruction.
Blue overlay: inferior vena cava; red overlay: abdominal aorta

Photomicrograph of a lymph node biopsy specimen (H&E stain, high magnification) A Reed–Sternberg cell (green overlay) can be seen in the center of the image. It is surrounded by numerous lymphocytes (examples indicated by arrowheads). This histopathological appearance is characteristic of Hodgkin lymphoma.

Right foot (dorsal view) Purplish discoloration of the toes and livedo reticularis of the back of the foot are visible. Blue toe syndrome is a manifestation of insufficient perfusion, usually caused by cholesterol embolization of an artery (typically after an invasive vascular procedure).

CT of the thorax (axial view) Bilateral hyperdensities in the areas of the mediastinal and hilar lymph nodes can be seen (green overlay).
There is also evidence of right-sided pleural effusion (red overlay). This is the typical appearance of bilateral lymphadenopathy, a common finding in sarcoidosis.

Macules of varying sizes with irregular, ill-defined borders and variable pigmentation (pink, brown). Some of these lesions feature a central papular component, which is responsible for the typical fried-egg appearance.

X-ray chest (PA view) of a patient following thoracic trauma A gas bubble on the left (green overlay) is positioned higher than normal and contains an air-fluid level (line). Additionally, the left hemidiaphragm is elevated, lobulated, and not sharply delineated in its entirety (dashed line). Elevation of a hemidiaphragm can result from abnormalities above, below, or involving the diaphragm. Diaphragmatic rupture can simulate elevation and must be considered in the differential diagnosis of elevation in a trauma setting. A CT scan of this patient confirmed diaphragmatic rupture with the stomach located in the left hemithorax.

Photomicrograph of a colon tissue sample (H&E stain) There are numerous nests of small, round, monomorphic cells, primarily arranged in rosette-like patterns (e.g., dashed outline, yellow central lumen). The cells show peripheral palisading of their nuclei.
Finely grained chromatin (salt-and-pepper chromatin, e.g., red overlay) can be seen in some cells. These histological findings are characteristic of a carcinoid tumor.

X-ray chest (PA view) of a patient with pleuritic chest pain A peripheral opacity (Hampton hump; green overlay) obscures the adjacent margin of the right hemidiaphragm. A Hampton hump is subpleural and is caused by pulmonary hemorrhage or infarction. The appearance is often wedge-shaped; however, it can alternatively be dome-shaped, if the apex is spared as a result of collateral bronchial arterial blood flow. The differential diagnosis includes pneumonia and malignancy. Of note, the greater distance between the spinous processes and the medial edge of the clavicle on the right compared to the left indicates that the patient is rotated slightly to the right. The distances will be nearly equal when a patient is not rotated, whereas widening will be seen on the side to which a patient is rotated. Dashed lines: margins of medial clavicles; White line and ellipses: spinous processes

X-ray (AP view) of an adolescent athlete An avulsion fracture of the lesser trochanter of the left femur is displaced superiorly (green overlay). The donor site of the left lesser trochanter shows an irregular contour (arrowheads) in contrast to the smooth contour of the right lesser trochanter. The lesser trochanter is the insertion site for the iliopsoas muscle.

CT head (without contrast; axial plane; brain window) A hyperdense, crescent-shaped extra-axial collection (green overlay) on the right side is accompanied by a contralateral shift of the midline structures. The appearance is characteristic of an acute subdural hematoma. White dashed line and arrow: midline; arrowhead: right lateral ventricle
An asymmetric nodule (approx. 1 cm in diameter) with central ulceration, and rolled-out edges is visible on the right medial canthus. This appearance is typical of nodular basal cell carcinoma.

ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Regular sinus rhythm with a heart rate of 37/min
– PR interval normal (∼ 160 ms), QRS normal (∼ 110 ms), QT interval normal (∼ 480 ms; QTc = 380 ms)
– Morphologies of P wave, QRS complex, and T wave are physiologic. Note the U waves (occurring after the T waves).
– No signs of ischemia

Photomicrograph of an endocervical biopsy specimen (H&E stain; high magnification) Atypical columnar epithelial cells with elongated nuclei are visible (green overlays). There is an abundance of neutrophils (as a sign of inflammation secondary to neoplasia; yellow overlays) within the subepithelial stroma indicating an invasive tumor. Parts of the invasive component can be seen in the center and at the bottom of the image (red overlays). This appearance is characteristic of endocervical adenocarcinoma.

Photograph of the medial aspect of the ankle Multiple dilated and tortuous veins are visible around the medial malleolus.
Numerous bluish telangiectasias can be seen toward the inferior spect, while reddish-brown pigmentation can be seen adjacent to the Achilles tendon.
These are typical features of chronic venous insufficiency due to varicose veins.
A confluent macular, erythematous rash is visible on both upper eyelids with concurrent periorbital edema. This is a characteristic finding in dermatomyositis.

X-ray abdomen (AP view) of a newborn with duodenal atresia A nasogastric tube terminates in the gastric antrum. Gaseous distention of the stomach and pyloric channel (green overlay) and dilatation of the duodenal bulb (red overlay) are seen. Bowel distal to the duodenal bulb does not contain gas. This appearance has been termed the “double-bubble sign.” This sign is seen with duodenal atresia and several other congenital and acquired causes of duodenal obstruction.

Fundus photography of the left eye There are diffuse perivascular retinal hemorrhages (examples indicated by green overlay), whitish retinal necroses (examples indicated by yellow overlay), and periphlebitis with vascular occlusion (examples indicated by blue lines). In addition, hard exudates (examples indicated by white arrowheads) can be seen. The combination of these features causes the characteristic pizza-pie appearance (white box and illustration). These findings are consistent with retinitis caused by cytomegalovirus, which is most commonly seen in immunocompromised patients.
Photomicrograph of a stool mount (trichrome stain) A trophozoite with phagocytosed erythrocytes (dark circular inclusions; examples indicated by arrowheads) can be seen.

The upper blue tracing shows the fetal heart rate (FHR) in beats per minute (bpm), with a baseline of approx. 135 bpm (green dashed line overlay). The FHR baseline lies within the normal range (green shading overlay) and exhibits moderate variability (∼ 10 bpm; normal range 6–25 bpm). The lower blue tracing shows maternal uterine contractions (in mm Hg). The black markings above this tracing show fetal movements (blue shading overlay). The two decelerations visible are abrupt, with < 30 s from onset to nadir (red arrow overlay). They moreover vary in their shape and their temporal relation to the uterine contractions (dashed red line overlay). These findings are characteristic of variable decelerations. As the decelerations here occur with < 50 % of uterine contractions over a 20-minute time period, they are termed intermittent variable decelerations.

X-ray pelvis (AP view) Deformity of the proximal right femur is the result of a displaced fracture through the femoral neck. The greater trochanter (GT; green overlay) projects over the femoral neck on this image and obscures the fracture line. Osteopenia and degenerative changes in the pelvis are also seen. Dashed outline and H: Femoral head; LT: Lesser trochanter

Tonsillopharyngitis caused by Epstein-Barr virus. Both tonsils are so massively enlarged that they touch, which is called kissing tonsils. They are covered with the characteristic white-grayish deposits (pus).
Violaceous nodules and papules are visible beside several black-crusted plaques on the lower leg of this patient with Kaposi sarcoma.
The entire hairless portion of the scalp is covered with telangiectasia, light brown papules, and yellow-brownish, hyperkeratotic plaques. This appearance is typical of actinic keratoses.

CT chest (with contrast; axial plane) The base-to-apex cardiac axis is directed to the patient's right (dextrocardia). The right ventricle (RV) and left ventricle (LV) are reversed. This patient also presented with right to left reversal of the morphologic atria and abdominal organs (situs inversus; not shown). Dextrocardia with situs inversus is part of the triad of manifestations seen in Kartagener syndrome, which also includes chronic sinusitis and bronchiectasis.

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) - Heart rate approx. 70/min - Regular sinus rhythm - Normal cardiac axis (R > S in I and aVF) - Normal narrow QRS complexes - Concave ST elevation in multiple leads (I, II, V2–6; red upwards arrows), with reciprocal ST depression in aVR (red downwards arrow). - PR segment depression in multiple leads (II, V2, V4–V6; blue downwards arrows) Upward concave ST elevation and PR segment depression in multiple leads (with reciprocal ST depression usually confined to aVR/V1) are characteristic of acute pericarditis.

X-ray chest (AP view) The cardiac silhouette is enlarged (hatched green overlay) and the perihilar air space opacities (green overlay) have a bat wing, or butterfly, configuration. Linear interstitial opacities representing Kerley A lines (orange dashed lines) radiate from the hila to the apices and Kerley B lines (white dashed lines) are seen in the lateral mid zones. The costophrenic angles are blunted (arrows) from bilateral pleural effusions. These features are characteristically seen in cardiogenic pulmonary edema.

X-ray pelvis (AP view) The right hemipelvis is displaced superiorly, compatible with disruption of the sacroiliac joint (indicated by dashed lines and double-headed arrow). Bilateral pubic ramus fractures are also present (indicated by arrowheads).

Bright-field photomicrograph (Gram stain; 1000x magnification) Large numbers of gram-positive bacteria are visible. The bacteria form pairs (examples indicated by arrowheads) and short chains (examples indicated by circles).

Right foot Darkened streaks are visible on the medial aspect of the big toe. The lesion extends into the proximal fold of the nailbed (Hutchinson sign). The Hutchinson sign is highly suggestive of subungual, acral lentiginous melanoma.

12-lead ECG (paper speed: 25 mm/s) – Atrial rate: ∼ 110/min
– Ventricular rate: ∼ 27/min (escape rhythm)
– QRS complex: ∼ 100 ms (normal)
– AV dissociation: P waves (black arrowheads) and QRS complexes (red arrowheads) both have regular rhythms, but the rhythms are unrelated because there is no conduction between the atria and ventricles. This results in some P waves being completely or partially buried in QRS complexes or T waves (green arrowheads). AV dissociation with bradycardia is characteristic of third-degree atrioventricular block (complete heart block).

Photomicrograph of a lung biopsy specimen (H&E stain; high magnification) A large epithelioid granuloma containing several multinucleated giant cells (Langhans cells; black arrowhead) is visible. Dense collections of leukocytes (examples indicated by black arrows) can be seen in the surrounding area. Necrotic areas (yellow overlay) are also visible. These findings are characteristic of a caseating tuberculous granuloma.
Ellipsoid scaling papules are visible at the level of the proximal interphalangeal joints (white arrows). These findings in a patient with dermatomyositis are consistent with Gottron papules.

Photomicrograph of a renal tumor tissue specimen (H&E stain; 400x magnification) A solid nest of tumor cells (without formation of glands or other structures) can be seen. The large tumor cells have a granular eosinophilic cytoplasm (examples indicated by arrowheads). Two atypical cells with hyperchromatic, pleomorphic nuclei (green overlay) are also visible. This is the characteristic appearance of a renal oncocytoma. It is a benign condition, even if a few degenerative cells with suspicious atypia are seen.

MRI head (T2-weighted; 2 slices in the axial plane) of a young child with known Chiari II malformation malformation The ventricles are markedly enlarged (green overlay) due to the obstructed passage of CSF at the craniocervical junction. The subarachnoid space appears unremarkable.
A sharply demarcated, pigmented macule with a stuck on appearance This appearance is consistent with seborrheic keratosis. Dermatoscopic examination should be performed to confirm the diagnosis.

Gross specimen of a spleen post infarction Multiple infarcts can be seen as lighter-colored areas of coagulative necrosis against the normal dark red splenic tissue. Splenic infarcts are classically subcapsular and wedge-shaped, following the spleen's segmental arterial supply (e.g., infarct in top left).
The sclerae show a distinct blue-grey tint.

Photomicrograph of a skin biopsy (H&E stain; very high magnification) Ovoid, dark purple inclusions (examples indicated by white arrows) are visible within macrophages. The nuclei (green overlay) appear normal. This appearance is typical of amastigotes, the intracellular, nonmotile form of the parasite Leishmania major.
There are multiple small, hyperpigmented papules distributed along the hair-growing areas of the neck.
Multiple sharply demarcated erythematosquamous plaques with coarse, silver-white scaling are visible on the extensor sides of the lower arms and elbows.
Extensive purple nodular lesions on the nose and cheeks Lupus pernio is a cutaneous manifestation of extrapulmonary sarcoidosis.

Vaginal wet mount after potassium hydroxide (KOH) preparation (microscopy) that shows Candida albicans forming multiple pseudohyphae (thin filamentous structures; examples indicated by blue overlay). The prominent grey and white circles are optical artifacts. White circles: examples of budding yeast
Typical clinical presentation of herpetic gingivostomatitis with multiple red erosions on the lower lip. Intact vesicles with surrounding erythema are found on the tongue.

Scaly, erythematous papules and periungual erythema are visible on the dorsal aspect of the interphalangeal and metacarpophalangeal joints. Both localization and appearance are typical of Gottron papules, which are pathognomonic of dermatomyositis.
Slit-lamp photography of a left eye A brown ring is visible in the periphery of the cornea (Kayser-Fleischer ring; green overlay). Kayser-Fleischer rings are typically seen in Wilson disease.

Trunk and right arm of an infant Multiple erythematous macules are visible, arranged in concentric rings with a pale center and an outer erythematous margin. The macules are partly confluent and of different sizes. These lesions are suggestive of erythema multiforme, a hypersensitivity reaction that can be caused by various infections (most commonly herpes simplex virus infections), certain drugs, or immune dysregulation.

(A) Polymerase III
(B) Single-strand binding protein
(C) Helicase
(D) Primase
(E) Topoisomerase II
(F) Polymerase I
(G) Ligase

Photomicrograph of a peripheral blood smear Multiple spherocytes can be seen (red circles), which have a typical round (rather than biconcave) shape and no central pallor. The erythrocytes vary significantly in size, which is consistent with anisocytosis (red arrows). On electron microscopy (overlay box with drawings based on EM appearance), the spherical shape of spherocytes (1) and normal biconcave erythrocytes (2) can be seen.

Blood smear (Giemsa stain, 100x magnification) The parasite Trypanosoma cruzi, the causative agent of Chagas disease, can be seen in the center of this blood smear (green overlay). Its characteristic flagellum (yellow overlay) is clearly recognizable.

Multiple light brown macules with irregular borders (café-au-lait spots) are visible on the upper left arm, shoulder, and back. This presence of multiple café-au-lait spots is highly suggestive of neurofibromatosis type 1.

(B) Aortic area

(D) Left sternal border

12-lead ECG (paper speed 25 mm/s) - Sinus rhythm with a heart rate of ∼78/min
- Normal heart axis (R > S in I and aVF)
- Normal PR interval (∼200 ms), narrow QRS complex (∼100 ms), normal QT interval (∼400 ms)
- ST elevation in II, III, and aVF (examples indicated in green) with reciprocal ST-depressions in lateral leads (I and aVL; examples indicated in red)

Transabdominal ultrasound of a pregnant patient A dichorionic diamniotic twin pregnancy in the first trimester is seen. The triangular appearance of the chorion insinuating between layers of the intertwin membrane resembles the Greek letter lambda (λ; blue overlay). The lambda sign (also known as the “twin peak sign”) strongly suggests a dichorionic twin pregnancy. Green overlay: placenta; red overlay: uterus; yellow overlay: fetus

Multiple well-defined erythematous skin lesions (purpura) are visible over both legs. Some purpura have coalesced to form ecchymoses, especially around the lower one-third of the legs. Examination revealed the purpura to be palpable and nonblanchable. Palpable, nonblanching symmetrically-distributed purpura predominantly clustered over gravity-dependent areas, such as the ankles, are characteristic features of Henoch-Schonlein purpura.

When the patient is asked to place both palms against a wall and push, the medial border of the right scapula is abnormally prominent. This is the characteristic appearance of scapular winging. It indicates a palsy of the serratus anterior, which is most commonly due to an injury of the long thoracic nerve.

Photograph of the anogenital region of an adult female patient Fluid-filled vesicles on erythematous bases are visible around the anus and vulva. Some of these vesicles have ruptured, leaving erythematous ulcerations. These findings are consistent with herpes genitalis, most likely caused by infection with herpes simplex virus type 2.

Dome-shaped, crusted, red nodule on the lower left eyelid of a child. Two smaller nodules can be seen above the left eye and on the forehead. These lesions are also referred to as strawberry hemangiomas and are usually located on the head and neck region.
